This work establishes the potential of simulation-assisted training for FM instance segmentation.

</details>


### [90] [Authentication of Copy Detection Patterns via Cross-Camera Dual-Synthetic Referencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31292)
*Ivan Oleksiyuk,Roman Chaban,Slava Voloshynovskiy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a new framework for authenticating Copy Detection Patterns (CDPs) against counterfeiting by addressing issues with printer stochasticity and camera distortions using an enrolment-based dual-synthetic referencing method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the robustness of CDP authentication against counterfeiting by improving the comparison between printed objects and digital templates, overcoming limitations posed by printer variability and camera distortions.

Method: The proposed method captures each printed CDP with a controlled enrolment camera and employs a deep-learning-based translator to produce a high-quality reference for verification by combining the digital template with the enrolled capture.

Result: The dual reference framework yields more informative comparisons, leading to improved authentication performance, robustness against machine-learning attacks, and reliable verification even from small CDP regions and on low-end devices.

Conclusion: This approach effectively improves the security and reliability of CDP authentication, making it feasible for various practical applications.

Abstract: Copy Detection Patterns (CDPs) are structures printed on physical objects to enable cost-effective authentication. Verification is achieved by comparing a captured image with the digital template from which the CDP was printed. In practice, printer stochasticity and camera distortions hinder this comparison, limiting robustness against counterfeiting. Prior work addressed camera effects by synthesising reference images in the verification camera domain, but it ignored printing variability. We introduce an enrolment-based cross-camera dual-synthetic referencing framework. Each printed CDP is first captured by a controlled enrolment camera, and a deep-learning-based translator jointly exploits the digital template and the enrolled capture to generate a high-quality reference for the verification image. We provide an information-theoretic justification showing that the dual reference is more informative than template-based references. Experiments on heterogeneous mobile cameras demonstrate improved authentication performance, robustness to machine-learning-based copy attacks, and reliable verification from small CDP regions and on low-end devices.

</details>


### [91] [TokTalk: Expressive Real-time Facial Animation from Audio-LLM Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31294)
*Qingcheng Zhao,Yifang Pan,Karan Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TokTalk is a real-time facial animation system that utilizes audio-tokens from Audio-LLMs to produce expressive facial performances, improving over traditional methods through efficient processing and adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance the expressiveness and naturalness of conversational avatars, which are typically limited by robotic facial expressions and unsophisticated conversational flow.

Method: TokTalk uses a novel dataset for training a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model to translate audio-tokens into 3D facial motion, featuring a lightweight adaptation strategy for integration with existing Audio-LLMs and a chunk-based processing approach to balance latency and facial quality.

Result: TokTalk demonstrates comparable latency to previous solutions while significantly outperforming them in terms of facial quality, expressivity, and user control through perceptual studies.

Conclusion: TokTalk successfully showcases a significant advancement in real-time facial animation for avatars, demonstrating versatility across various audio-visual applications.

Abstract: Recent advances in Audio-LLMs like GPT-4o have ushered in an era of conversational interaction with language models. Conversational avatars however, still seem robotic in facial expression and conversational flow, in part due to sequential stages of speech recognition, text generation, turn-based text response, speech synthesis, and audio driven facial animation. Based on our insight that audio-tokens produced by current Audio-LLMs carry sufficient information to reconstruct a plausible facial performance, we present TokTalk, a system that directly outputs expressive facial animation in real-time from streaming audio-tokens. We construct a novel audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset, on which TokTalk is trained using a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model. A lightweight adaptation strategy allows our trained model to seamlessly connect to any token-based Audio-LLM at minimal computational overhead. Our chunk-based processing further enables parametric trade-off between latency and facial quality, shown through ablation studies. We further show that the real-time performance of TokTalk is comparable in latency to prior art solutions, and significantly favorable (via a perceptual study) in terms of quality, expressivity and control of the 3D facial performance. We showcase TokTalk's flexibility using a chatbot Avatar, a voice-driven user Avatar, and an animation Director's interface, as diverse audio-visual face applications.

</details>


### [92] [DecMem: Towards Minute-Long Consistent World Generation with Decoupled Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31336)
*Zhenhao Yang,Xiaoshi Wu,Zhengyao Lv,Xiaoyu Shi,Xintao Wang,Pengfei Wan,Kun Gai,Kwan-Yee K. Wong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DecMem is a novel decoupled memory architecture for video generative models that enhances long-horizon reasoning through efficient and consistent memory utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of maintaining fine-grained spatio-temporal consistency in long-horizon video generation, which current approaches struggle with.

Method: The proposed DecMem architecture uses Sparse Global Memory for accessing global history effectively and Anchored Local Memory for stable extrapolation, addressing the limitations of existing memory architectures.

Result: DecMem demonstrates significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of long-term memory efficiency and extrapolation quality.

Conclusion: By improving memory architecture, DecMem facilitates the generation of long-duration videos with enhanced control, fidelity, and consistency.

Abstract: Recent advances in video generative models have promoted rapid progress in controllable world models. However, maintaining fine-grained spatio-temporal consistency under long-horizon reasoning remains a key challenge. In this work, we move beyond explicit 3D memory and coarse frame-level implicit modeling, and propose a fine-grained, learnable, and scalable memory for consistent world generation. We first identify two fundamental limitations of naïve learnable memory architectures in long-horizon extrapolation, namely computational inefficiency and attention dispersion. Through a systematic analysis of attention dispersion, we propose DecMem, a decoupled memory architecture that employs Sparse Global Memory for efficient fine-grained access to global history and Anchored Local Memory for stable and high-quality extrapolation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DecMem significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods. By ensuring precise and efficient long-term memory and achieving superior extrapolation capabilities, DecMem enables minute-level controllable long video generation with high fidelity and consistency.

</details>


### [93] [Learning from Fine-Grained Visual Discrepancies: Mitigating Multimodal Hallucinations via In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31312)
*Haolin Deng,Xin Zou,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Xuming Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a new method, In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization (IC-VCO), to address multimodal hallucination in Vision-Language Models by using a rigorous contrastive learning approach and auxiliary regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate the issue of multimodal hallucination in Vision-Language Models, which is not effectively addressed by existing Direct Preference Optimization methods due to theoretical inconsistencies and reliance on coarse negative samples.

Method: IC-VCO contrasts images within a shared multi-image context to ensure a consistent objective, while Visual Contrast Distillation (VCDist) serves as an auxiliary regularizer to enhance consistency between training and inference. Additionally, a contrastive sample editing strategy is implemented to generate hard negatives through semantic perturbations.

Result: Experiments on five benchmarks show that IC-VCO achieves the best overall performance compared to existing methods, validating the effectiveness of the proposed contrastive sample editing strategy.

Conclusion: The proposed IC-VCO method provides a mathematically sound approach to reduce multimodal hallucination and outperforms existing techniques in various benchmarks.

Abstract: Multimodal hallucination remains a persistent challenge for Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Standard textual Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) often fails to mitigate it due to a lack of explicit visual supervision. While existing works introduce visual preference DPO by contrasting original images against negative ones, they suffer from a theoretically inconsistent objective caused by partition function mismatches and rely on coarse-grained negatives that could enable shortcut learning. In this work, we propose In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization (IC-VCO). By placing contrastive images within a shared multi-image context, IC-VCO ensures a mathematically rigorous objective. We further introduce Visual Contrast Distillation (VCDist), an auxiliary reliability-gated regularizer that encourages consistency between multi-image contrastive training and single-image inference. Finally, we propose a contrastive sample editing strategy that generates hard negatives via precise semantic perturbations. Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate IC-VCO's best overall performance and the effectiveness of our sample editing strategy. Code and data are available at https://github.com/OPPO-Mente-Lab/IC-VCO.

</details>


### [94] [YARD: Y-Architecture Register Decoding for Efficient Hallucination Mitigation in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31429)
*Ting Chen,Geng Li,Guohao Chen,Yu Hu,Guan Huang,Mai Chen,Langsheng Lei,Jun Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: YARD is a training-free framework that improves hallucination mitigation in LVLMs by constructing a degraded branch at critical decoder layers, reducing inference latency while maintaining effective contrastive decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current training-free CD methods that lead to hallucinations and high inference latency in LVLMs.

Method: YARD employs a Y-Architecture Register Decoding, leveraging shared shallow-layer computations and replacing visual tokens with register tokens in the degraded branch.

Result: YARD achieves state-of-the-art performance in hallucination mitigation on various benchmarks while significantly lowering inference latency compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed YARD framework effectively balances the need for visual grounding and computational efficiency, improving the decoding process in LVLMs.

Abstract: Contrastive decoding (CD) seeks to mitigate hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) by contrasting the output distributions of a standard model and a visually degraded model. However, existing training-free CD methods suffer from sub-optimal degraded branches: completely dropping visual tokens is too extreme and induces language hallucinations, while corrupting input images offers coarse control over visual evidence and suffers from high inference latency due to requiring two full forward passes. To address these dilemmas, we propose YARD, a training-free Y-Architecture Register Decoding framework. Motivated by the observation that reliable text-to-vision grounding predominantly emerges in the middle decoder layers, YARD constructs the degraded branch internally by sharing shallow-layer computations and branching exactly at this critical stage. For the degraded branch, YARD replaces patch-level visual tokens with register tokens, which preserve global image semantics but lack fine-grained local evidence. This image-aware yet locally under-grounded design provides a faithful contrastive signal without extreme modality mismatch, while the Y-architecture strictly avoids a costly second forward pass. Extensive experiments on generative and discriminative hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that YARD consistently achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation across multiple LVLMs, alongside a significant reduction in inference latency.

</details>


### [95] [Astra: a generalizable report generation foundation model for 3D computed tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31437)
*Zhuhao Wang,Fang Chen,Chaohui Yu,Zihan Li,Yuchao Zheng,Jing Wang,Xuan Yang,Jia Guo,Zhenlu Yang,Xingju Zheng,Yihua Sun,Haojie Han,Xiaoxiao Qin,Zhan Feng,Wenbo Xiao,Chao Zhu,Yuehua Li,Shipeng Zhang,Hao Luo,Yunsong Peng,Fan Wang,Hongen Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Astra is a novel CT report generation model that enhances reporting efficiency and accuracy by harmonizing reporting style and refining diagnostic consistency across multiple cohorts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for efficient CT report generation due to the high volume of slices and reliance on expert radiologists.

Method: Astra was developed using a dataset of 90,678 CT-report pairs, utilizing reinforcement learning to standardize reporting styles and improve diagnosis consistency.

Result: Astra shows a 44.1% improvement in diagnostic metrics, accelerates report drafting by 29.6%, and enhances completeness by 11.3% in clinical workflows.

Conclusion: Astra improves clinical efficiency and accuracy, establishing itself as a vital tool for future AI applications in healthcare.

Abstract: CT interpretation requires radiologists to review hundreds of volumetric slices per examination, making reporting time-consuming and highly expertise-dependent. Automated CT report generation offers a promising route to improving clinical efficiency, yet the field still lacks a generalizable CT report generation foundation model that supports multi-region reporting and remains robust across external real-world cohorts. Intrinsic inconsistencies in reporting style and diagnostic terminology across cohorts make naive joint training prone to noisy textual supervision, thereby limiting model generalizability. Here we present Astra, a generalizable CT report generation foundation model trained on 90,678 thoracoabdominal CT-report pairs (CTRgDB) with 353,671 abnormalities spanning eight organ systems. By harmonizing report style and further refining diagnostic consistency via reinforcement learning, Astra achieves style-consistent and diagnostically accurate report generation across diverse anatomical regions and institutions. Evaluating on CTRgDB and six external cohorts, Astra achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 44.1% average improvement in fine-grained diagnostic metrics (P<0.001). In real-world clinical workflows, Astra assistance accelerates chest report drafting by 29.6% and improves abdominal report completeness by 11.3% (P<0.001). Furthermore, Astra also demonstrates broad utility as a foundation for CT AI development, improving downstream diagnostic performance and scaling vision-language pretrain through high-quality report synthesis. Overall, Astra serves as a broadly accessible clinical assistant and a pivotal infrastructure for the next generation of AI-powered healthcare.

</details>


### [96] [VisionPulse: Dynamic Visual Sparsity for Efficient Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31457)
*Hengbo Xu,Shengjie Jin,Yanbiao Ma,Zhiwu Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VisionPulse reduces inference-time overhead in large multimodal models by implementing step-wise visual token pruning, significantly shortening reasoning traces while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to address the inference-time overhead in LMMs, as existing methods do not adapt to the dynamic nature of visual evidence required during reasoning.

Method: VisionPulse utilizes a step-wise visual token pruning framework that computes a visual attention mass to prioritize the most critical visual tokens at each decoding step.

Result: VisionPulse retains 5% of visual tokens per step and shortens reasoning traces by 11.2%, achieving this with minimal impact on accuracy.

Conclusion: By implementing step-wise pruning, VisionPulse effectively enhances the efficiency of LMMs in real-world applications.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large multimodal models (LMMs), inference-time overhead has become a key bottleneck for real-world deployment. Existing methods typically prune visual tokens at prefill, assuming the required visual evidence remains static during reasoning. However, we empirically show that visual evidence is strongly step-dependent: only a sparse subset of visual tokens is critical at each decoding step, and the critical set evolves across reasoning. Furthermore, we identify a coupled bottleneck where redundant visual context can steer the model toward query-irrelevant regions, lengthening the reasoning trace. Guided by these insights, we propose VisionPulse, a step-wise visual token pruning framework during reasoning. VisionPulse computes a lightweight visual attention mass to estimate the step-wise retention budget by exploiting its strong positive correlation with LMMs' effective visual token usage and retain only the most critical tokens under this budget. By enforcing visual sparsity during reasoning, VisionPulse filters redundant visual context while preserving relevant visual evidence, shortening reasoning traces naturally. Extensive experiments show that VisionPulse only retains 5% of visual tokens per step with reasoning traces shortened by 11.2%, while keeping accuracy almost unchanged.

</details>


### [97] [VolFill: Single-View Amodal 3D Scene Reconstruction with Volumetric Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31466)
*Tuan Duc Ngo,Chuang Gan,Evangelos Kalogerakis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VolFill is a generative framework for reconstructing 3D scenes from single RGB images, outperforming traditional methods by compressing data into a latent space and utilizing a Diffusion Transformer for denoising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the reconstruction of 3D scenes where visual evidence is incomplete, addressing the limitations of existing methods that rely on pixel-aligned regression and face challenges with hidden structures.

Method: VolFill employs a hybrid 3D VAE to encode sparse distance function grids into a latent space, which is further processed by a latent Diffusion Transformer to denoise and reconstruct the complete 3D scene.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that VolFill significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets, offering enhanced surface extraction and occupancy query capabilities.

Conclusion: VolFill provides a robust foundation for holistic spatial understanding, overcoming limitations faced by current approaches in 3D scene reconstruction.

Abstract: Reconstructing the complete geometry of a scene from a single RGB image remains challenging - especially when inferring hidden structures where visual evidence is incomplete. We introduce VolFill, a generative framework that predicts the 3D structure of the complete scene rather than relying on traditional pixel-aligned regression. Our method utilizes a hybrid 3D VAE to compress sparse truncated unsigned distance function grids into a compact latent space, paired with a latent Diffusion Transformer that denoises this representation to recover the complete scene. We condition the generation on geometry foundation models, leveraging rich spatial priors for robust reasoning. Unlike existing methods limited by per-ray constraints or unstructured point-cloud queries, VolFill provides a structured representation that supports direct surface extraction and occupancy queries at scale. Extensive experiments on the SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms current baselines, providing a robust foundation for holistic spatial understanding.

</details>


### [98] [How can embedding models bind concepts?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31503)
*Arnas Uselis,Darina Koishigarina,Seong Joon Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates the limitations of vision-language models like CLIP in concept binding within multi-object scenes, revealing that while current models struggle with binding complexity, simpler transformer models can generalize effectively with adequate data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the concept binding ability in multi-object scenes and the limitations of vision-language models like CLIP.

Method: The study employs an analysis of the binding function in vision-language models, examining how scene embeddings relate to object representations and comparing complexity in binding functions between CLIP and controlled transformer models.

Result: Scene embeddings can be decomposed additively into object representations; however, CLIP’s high-complexity binding functions hinder its generalization capabilities.

Conclusion: Binding generalization is not a fundamental limitation of the binding task, as simpler transformer models can achieve effective generalization with sufficient data coverage.

Abstract: Humans easily determine which color belongs to which shape in multi-object scenes, an ability known as concept binding. Vision-language embedding models such as CLIP struggle with binding: they recognize individual concepts but fail to represent which concepts form which objects. Although CLIP behaves like a bag-of-concepts model in cross-modal retrieval, object information is recoverable from its image and text embeddings separately. We study this tension through the binding function, which maps concepts to scene embeddings. We find that scene embeddings decompose additively into object representations, explaining why uni-modal probes can recover object information. However, CLIP's binding function is high-complexity, which likely prevents the image and text encoders from learning a shared binding mechanism that generalizes to unseen concept combinations. We then ask whether this limitation is fundamental. We show that it is not. In controlled transformer models trained from scratch, binding generalization emerges with sufficient data coverage. These models learn low-complexity binding functions characterized by multiplicative interactions between concepts, enabling systematic generalization. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/oshapio/binding-concepts-complexity.

</details>


### [99] [Enhancing Computer Vision Model Generalization in Warehouse Facilities: A Case Study on Anomaly Detection in Vertical Material Handling Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31487)
*Ruiliang Liu,Tina Dongxu Li,Joshua Migdal,Ken Meszaros,Trevor Dardik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a method to optimize the deployment of computer vision models in warehouses by conducting the necessary procedures in a lab setting, thus reducing resources needed in field settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the resource-intensive process of deploying computer vision models in diverse warehouse environments, which typically require extensive camera setup and repeated model training.

Method: The study utilizes optimal camera placement, strategic image triggering, model selection, and model ensemble in a laboratory setting to prepare for deployment in actual warehouses.

Result: Experimental findings demonstrate that this approach allows for effective generalization of models from lab conditions to various warehouse environments, significantly reducing the need for additional resources in deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed method could revolutionize warehouse automation by simplifying the camera setup and model deployment process, ultimately saving time and resources.

Abstract: Deploying computer vision models in Warehouse Facilities traditionally requires extensive resources for camera mounting, image collection, annotation, training, and deployment - a process often needing repetition in each new environment due to camera mounting constraints and environmental variability. This paper explores an innovative approach to streamline this process by conducting the standard procedure solely in a laboratory setting, focusing on vertical material handling systems and anomaly detection in forks of the systems. Through extensive experimentation, we have found that combining optimal camera placement, strategic image triggering, careful model selection and model ensemble enables effective generalization from laboratory conditions to diverse warehouse facilities environments, potentially transforming warehouse automation implementation by simplifying warehouse facilities deployment to just camera mounting, image collection, and model deployment, thereby saving significant resources and time typically spent on image annotation and model retraining. This is an experimental research study and not a production deployment.

</details>


### [100] [Internalizing Temporal Consistency in Video Object-Centric Learning without Explicit Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31508)
*Rongzhen Zhao,Zhiyuan Li,Juho Kannala,Joni Pajarinen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a paradigm shift in Video Object-Centric Learning (OCL) by replacing Slot-Slot Contrastive (SSC) loss with the xSSC framework, enhancing temporal consistency through implicit mechanisms without additional loss penalties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortcomings of the Slot-Slot Contrastive loss in enforcing object correspondence across video frames while simplifying the model design.

Method: The xSSC framework employs Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) to separate slot representations into static and dynamic sub-spaces, and Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) to reconstruct features across time steps with a standard OCL decoder, thereby removing SSC reliance.

Result: The integration of xSSC into leading video OCL baselines yields improved training efficiency and new state-of-the-art results in video object discovery and recognition tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed mechanisms effectively encode time-invariant and time-variant semantics, enhancing learning without the explicit SSC loss, as supported by extensive experiments and analyses.

Abstract: Video Object-Centric Learning (OCL) aims to represent objects as \textit{slot} vectors and maintain their consistency across frames. Slot-Slot Contrastive (SSC) loss has become the cornerstone for state-of-the-art (SOTA) video OCL methods. While highly effective, SSC relies on one-to-one object correspondence across frames and introduces an extra loss. Following Occam's Razor, we propose a paradigm shift: temporal consistency is better enforced as an implicit model design rather than an explicit loss. To elegantly exclude SSC (\textbf{xSSC}), we introduce two quasi-zero-overhead synergistic mechanisms: (\textit{i}) Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) structurally disentangles slot representations along the channel dimension into \textit{static} and \textit{dynamic} sub-spaces, serving as an empirically unified information bottleneck; (\textit{ii}) Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) stochastically reconstructs target features of either the current or previous time step by fusing current slots' static channels and target slots' dynamic channels, using a single standard OCL decoder with minor training adaptation. Thereby, the slot sets inherently learn temporal consistency by minimizing the standard reconstruction error alone. Extensive experiments show that integrating xSSC into leading baselines not only improves training efficiency but also establishes new SOTAs on video object discovery and recognition tasks. Furthermore, our PCA and gradient analyses confirm that objects' time-invariant semantics and time-variant kinematics are encoded into the proposed sub-spaces. Our source code, model checkpoints and training logs are provided on https://github.com/Genera1Z/xSSC.

</details>


### [101] [Personalize Your Large Vision-language Models With In-context Prompt Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31513)
*Yanshu Li,Jiaqian Li,Kuai Yu,Xi Xiao,Dongfang Liu,Tianyang Wang,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents in-context prompt tuning (ICPT) for effective LVLM personalization, addressing the limitations of existing methods through a lightweight projection module and novel geometric regularizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for efficient and accurate personalization of large vision-language models (LVLMs) in real-world applications drives this research.

Method: ICPT employs a lightweight projection module that extracts fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images, adapting prompt lengths based on visual complexity and utilizing geometric regularizations to improve accuracy and reduce biases.

Result: ICPT achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy in diverse multimodal tasks and across LVLM backbones, overcoming existing challenges in complex scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed method enhances the deployment of LVLM-based systems by efficiently personalizing multimodal understanding while mitigating biases and improving accuracy.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated strong general multimodal capability and are increasingly deployed in downstream systems. This trend has driven growing interest in LVLM personalization, which aims to enable models to quickly and effectively learn out-of-distribution multimodal concepts to meet user-specific needs. However, many existing methods rely on inference-time training, which reduces efficiency. They also struggle to maintain accuracy in complex multi-image, multi-concept settings. These limitations restrict the broader deployment of LVLM-based systems. Therefore, this paper proposes in-context prompt tuning (ICPT). Specifically, ICPT employs a lightweight projection module capable of operating in complex scenarios to extract fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images, seamlessly transforming these features alongside identity-label mappings into continuous prompts. To maximize computational efficiency, this module adaptively determines the prompt length based on the intrinsic visual complexity of each concept. Crucially, to overcome the environmental biases and cross-concept interference prevalent in real-world applications, we introduce two novel geometric regularizations. These constraints refine prompt representations by decoupling key identities from transient environmental states and separating concepts to avoid semantic confusion. Extensive experiments show that ICPT achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones.

</details>


### [102] [SVI-Bench: A Dynamic Microworld for Strategic Video Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31529)
*Yulu Pan,Han Yi,Seongsu Ha,Md Mohaiminul Islam,Benjamin Zhang,Lorenzo Torresani,Gedas Bertasius*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents SVI-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate Strategic Video Intelligence through team sports, combining real-world complexity with verifiable outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of existing benchmarks to assess the capabilities involved in causal reasoning and strategic planning for video intelligence, particularly in complex, multi-agent scenarios.

Method: SVI-Bench utilizes team sports as a dynamic microworld to create a large-scale dataset that features extensive annotated actions, expert commentary, and game reports across multiple sports, organizing evaluations into four cognitive levels.

Result: Evaluation of multimodal models reveals a significant drop in performance from perceptual tasks to agentic tasks, with the strongest model achieving only 5% accuracy on complex agentic tasks.

Conclusion: The study highlights the need for improved methodologies in video intelligence, especially in the areas of causal reasoning and strategic planning, as demonstrated by the stark performance gap in different cognitive levels.

Abstract: True video intelligence demands more than recognizing what is visible: it requires reasoning about why events unfold, predicting what would change under different conditions, and deciding what to do next. We refer to this progression, from perception through causal reasoning and simulation to strategic planning, as Strategic Video Intelligence (SVI). No existing benchmark evaluates this capability stack: in-the-wild videos lack verifiable ground truth for causal and strategic questions, while synthetic environments sacrifice the complexity of real multi-agent systems. To bridge this gap, we introduce SVI-Bench, a large-scale benchmark that leverages team sports as a dynamic microworld, combining the complexity of real-world multi-agent interaction (10-22 agents making coordinated decisions under adversarial pressure) with the verifiability of explicit rules and definitive outcomes. SVI-Bench comprises approximately 35K hours of broadcast video, 15M annotated actions, 15K hours of expert commentary, 23K game reports, and 103K structured statistical records across basketball, soccer, and hockey, all constructed via a data engine that transforms raw game data into a dense, cross-referenced corpus. We organize evaluation into 9 tasks spanning a progressive four-pillar hierarchy: Dynamic Scene Understanding, Causal Reasoning, Strategic Simulation, and Agentic Synthesis. Evaluating strong multimodal and agentic baselines, we find a capability cliff: models perform competently on perceptual tasks, achieving approximately 73% on fine-grained action QA, but degrade sharply at each successive cognitive level. Agentic tasks prove hardest: the strongest model achieves only 5% accuracy when required to autonomously gather and integrate evidence across a corpus of 1.8M clips.

</details>


### [103] [Feature-Optimized Vision for Adaptive 3D Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31534)
*Eric Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces an adaptive feature-optimized vision front end for 3D reconstruction that improves efficiency and quality by selectively allocating computational resources to candidate image features based on their scoring criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance 3D scene reconstruction by optimizing the computational allocation to features, avoiding waste on less informative image data.

Method: The proposed method evaluates candidate features based on multiple criteria, including texture and spatial coverage, and assigns a tailored feature budget for each view.

Result: In synthetic tests, the adaptive feature selection policy outperformed baseline methods in terms of reconstruction quality and efficiency, achieving better completeness and lower RMSE.

Conclusion: This approach serves as a modular enhancement for existing 3D reconstruction systems, aiming to optimize visual evidence utilization without replacing current learning-based methods.

Abstract: Three-dimensional scene reconstruction depends on local image evidence that is both visually discriminative and geometrically useful. Fixed feature thresholds and uniform feature budgets are easy to deploy, but they can waste computation on repeated texture, low-parallax regions, or unstable points. This paper proposes an adaptive feature-optimized vision front end for 3D reconstruction. The method scores candidate features by texture, repeatability, distinctiveness, expected triangulation angle, and spatial coverage, then allocates a per-view feature budget to maximize useful tracks under a fixed reconstruction pipeline. A small synthetic multi-view prototype evaluates four selection policies across corridor, facade, object-table, and cluttered scenes. Compared with random, texture-only, and uniform-grid baselines, the adaptive policy obtains the best quality-aware completeness and the lowest aggregate reconstruction RMSE while preserving broad image coverage. The result is not a replacement for modern learned matching or neural reconstruction systems; it is a modular front-end policy that can make classical and learned 3D pipelines more deliberate about which visual evidence they spend compute on.

</details>


### [104] [RayDer: Scalable Self-Supervised Novel View Synthesis from Real-World Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31535)
*Ulrich Prestel,Stefan Andreas Baumann,Nick Stracke,Björn Ommer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RayDer is a unified transformer model for self-supervised novel view synthesis (NVS) that consolidates multiple tasks into a single architecture, facilitating stable training and showcasing robust scaling properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenges of scaling self-supervised NVS with realistic video data and the unpredictable performance of multi-network systems.

Method: RayDer is a feed-forward transformer that integrates camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering into one model, utilizing minimal dynamic states to manage time-varying content.

Result: RayDer demonstrates effective power-law scaling with both data and computational resources, outperforming static-scene data mixtures and achieving strong performances on various benchmarks.

Conclusion: RayDer's design offers a viable solution for scaling self-supervised NVS while maintaining strong performance in zero-shot and open-set scenarios, competing with state-of-the-art supervised methods.

Abstract: Self-supervised novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging to scale, despite the abundance of video data, largely due to the brittleness of training on realistic videos and the hard-to-predict scaling behavior of multi-network system designs. We introduce RayDer, a unified, feed-forward transformer that consolidates camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering into a single backbone, turning self-supervised NVS into a well-posed single-model scaling problem. A minimal dynamic state, treated as a nuisance factor, absorbs time-varying content and enables stable training on unconstrained real-world video. Importantly, RayDer keeps static-scene NVS as its target task: dynamic content is leveraged purely as scalable supervision, not reconstructed as in dynamic-scene (4D) NVS. Across multiple model sizes and orders of magnitude in data, RayDer exhibits clean power-law scaling with data and compute, and outperforms static-scene data mixtures. On a large number of benchmarks, RayDer achieves strong zero-shot open-set performance competitive with state-of-the-art supervised approaches. Project Page: https://compvis.github.io/rayder

</details>


### [105] [Automated Prediction of Postoperative Pancreatic Fistula Using Preoperative Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31539)
*Ashok Choudhary,Chris Varghese,Leo Y. Li-Han,Frank G. Lee,Ellen L. Larson,Elizabeth B. Habermann,Cornelius A. Thiels,Hojjat Salehinejad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The study develops a deep learning pipeline for preoperative risk estimation of postoperative pancreatic fistula using CT scans and various 3D CNN architectures, showing promising predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Postoperative pancreatic fistula is a serious complication impacting recovery and costs, highlighting the need for effective preoperative risk assessment.

Method: An automatic deep learning pipeline was created involving pancreatic segmentation and classification using preoperative CT scans, evaluated on a dataset with auto-segmented pancreas volumes.

Result: Multiple 3D CNN architectures, including a custom lightweight model, exhibited promising predictive performance for preoperative POPF risk.

Conclusion: This approach provides a valuable clinical tool and methodological benchmark for enhancing preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

Abstract: Postoperative pancreatic fistula (POPF) is a serious complication after pancreatic resection, increasing morbidity, hospital stay, and healthcare costs. We present an automatic, end-to-end deep learning pipeline-from pancreatic segmentation to classification-for preoperative POPF risk estimation and stratification using preoperative CT scans. A data set with auto-segmented pancreas volumes and surgical outcomes was used to evaluate multiple architectures, including a custom lightweight 3D CNN baseline (CNN3D), R(2+1)D ResNet-18, and ResNet-MC3-18 models. Evaluation across multiple 3D architectures demonstrated promising predictive performance. This approach offers a clinically valuable tool and a methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification, supporting improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

</details>


### [106] [Vision-Language Models Suppress Female Representations Under Ambiguous Input](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31556)
*Arnau Marin-Llobet,Simon Henniger,Mahzarin R. Banaji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper examines gender bias in vision-language models (VLMs) using ambiguous inputs, revealing discrepancies between internal associations and outputs, along with a novel metric, LALS, to analyze these findings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how vision-language models handle gender biases in ambiguous scenarios, particularly in real-world applications where demographic stereotypes may influence outputs.

Method: The study utilizes a metric called LALS to analyze internal representations of VLMs by projecting visual-token activations into text-embedding space across various layers, assessing their gender associations.

Result: Findings show that while models may internally associate with female occupations, they often output male representations. The analysis indicates a male signal amplification leading to biased outputs.

Conclusion: The internal representations of VLMs regarding gender are complex and not always reflected in their outputs; visual cues play a significant role in shaping these associations.

Abstract: Alignment teaches vision-language models (VLMs) to avoid expressing demographic biases, and when gender is clearly visible they largely succeed. Far less is known about ambiguous inputs (a worker in full gear, a figure seen from behind) cases common in practice yet rarely studied. We find that minimal prompting pressure exposes occupation-gender defaults when prompting ambiguous input images, with models collapsing to male even for strongly female-stereotyped occupations. But do these outputs reflect what models actually encode internally? We introduce LALS (Latent Association Leaning Score), a zero-shot metric that projects visual-token activations into the model's text-embedding space to measure concept associations per token and layer. Across 15 occupations, over 800 gender-ambiguous images, and four VLMs, internal representations and outputs are systematically decoupled: models often encode a female association internally yet output male. Layer-wise analysis reveals an asymmetric filter -- male signal amplifies end-to-end while female signal peaks mid-network and is suppressed before generation -- and a color ablation shows that culturally loaded visual cues such as clothing color further modulate these internal associations.

</details>


### [107] [EGOSTREAM: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Streaming Episodic Memory in Egocentric Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31557)
*Rosario Forte,Giuseppe Lando,Antonino Furnari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: egostream is a diagnostic benchmark for streaming episodic memory evaluation in egocentric vision, introducing 2,250 questions across seven cognitive dimensions and the Answer Validity Window (AVW) for controlled memory testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current streaming video benchmarks in diagnosing the memory capabilities of autonomous agents.

Method: Introduced egostream benchmark with 2,250 curated questions, expanded to 8,528 evaluations, and established baseline performances using a unified streaming MLLM framework comparing various memory management mechanisms.

Result: Experiments revealed significant differences in memory profiles among various mechanisms despite similar aggregate accuracies, with token pruning and quantized offloading showing notable advantages for different recall durations.

Conclusion: Current memory-management architectures fall short of real-time processing and achieve limited accuracy, highlighting the need for improvements, as demonstrated by egostream.

Abstract: Continuous episodic memory is a core capability for autonomous agents operating in dynamic, real-world environments, yet current streaming video benchmarks provide limited tools for diagnosing what models remember and for how long. We introduce \egostream, a diagnostic benchmark for streaming episodic memory evaluation in egocentric vision. \egostream organizes 2,250 curated questions along seven cognitive dimensions: detail, spatial, temporal, event, social, causal, and prospective memory. We introduce the Answer Validity Window (AVW), which specifies the temporal span an answer remains valid as the observed scene evolves. This allows us to expand the questions into 8,528 recall-conditioned evaluations, enabling controlled testing from instant to ultra-long-term recall while separating genuine model forgetting from natural world-state changes. We rigorously establish baseline performance through a unified streaming MLLM framework that compares several state-of-the-art memory-management mechanisms, covering sliding windows, attention sinks, KV-cache pruning, merging, and offloading. Experiments within a unified Qwen3-VL backbone reveal that comparable aggregate accuracies mask starkly different memory profiles. For instance, token pruning preserves fine-grained details and temporal structure significantly better than token merging, while quantized offloading rescues ultra-long-term recall. Ultimately, all mechanisms operate well below real-time (>1s per frame), and top performing methods ceil at about 45\% accuracy, exposing critical gaps in current architectures. \egostream provides the diagnostic testbed needed to close these gaps.

</details>


### [108] [nuReasoning: A Reasoning-Centric Dataset and Benchmark for Long-Tail Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31572)
*Zhiyu Huang,Johnson Liu,Rui Song,Zewei Zhou,Ruining Yang,Yun Zhang,Tianhui Cai,Hanyin Zhang,Mingxuan Gao,Valeria Xu,Jiali Chen,Yishan Shen,Yiluan Guo,Tony,Qi,Jiaqi Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: nuReasoning is a large-scale dataset for reasoning in autonomous driving, addressing limitations in existing datasets by providing diverse real-world scenarios and reasoning evaluation frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance autonomous driving capabilities in long-tail scenarios by providing a comprehensive dataset that supports reasoning, which is often overlooked in current datasets focused on perception and prediction.

Method: The nuReasoning dataset comprises 20,000 clips with synchronized data types, including multi-camera images, LiDAR, and human-verified reasoning annotations for multiple reasoning types.

Result: Fine-tuning visual language models on nuReasoning improved performance in driving-specific question answering, and integrating reasoning supervision enhanced planning even without text outputs during inference.

Conclusion: nuReasoning serves as a critical resource for developing reasoning-driven autonomous driving systems, enabling better decision-making in complex driving environments.

Abstract: Reasoning is essential for autonomous driving (AD) in long-tail scenarios, where vehicles must apply commonsense knowledge, understand spatial relations, infer agent interactions, and make safe decisions. However, existing AD datasets and benchmarks mainly target perception, prediction, or planning, and provide limited supervision for reasoning over realistic long-tail driving scenes. We introduce nuReasoning, a large-scale real-world dataset and benchmark for reasoning-centric AD. Following the lineage of nuScenes and nuPlan, nuReasoning advances real-world AD datasets and benchmarks toward reasoning in long-tail driving scenarios. The dataset contains 20,000 clips, each 20 seconds long, collected across multiple cities, with synchronized multi-camera images, LiDAR data, HD maps, object annotations, and human-verified reasoning annotations spanning Spatial Reasoning, Decision Reasoning, and Counterfactual Reasoning. Unlike prior datasets that focus primarily on visual question answering, nuReasoning supports both reasoning evaluation and planning evaluation, enabling a direct study of how reasoning supervision affects driving performance. Experiments show that fine-tuning VLMs on nuReasoning substantially improves driving-specific question answering, while incorporating reasoning supervision into VLA training improves planning performance even when textual reasoning outputs are disabled at inference time. These results establish nuReasoning as a foundation for evaluating and improving robust, interpretable, reasoning-driven AD systems in realistic long-tail settings.

</details>


### [109] [Joint Multi-Camera LiDAR Extrinsic Calibration via Learned Pairwise Initialization and Geometric Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31576)
*Aziz Al-Najjar,Marzieh Amini,James R. Green,Felix Kwamena*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a dual-stage framework for joint multi-camera LiDAR extrinsic calibration to enhance the consistency and accuracy of calibration estimates across multiple cameras, counteracting previous methods that independently calibrate each camera.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based methods for camera-LiDAR calibration do not consider the rigid geometric relationships in multi-camera systems, leading to potential inconsistencies in overall calibration despite individual accuracy.

Method: The proposed framework consists of applying CMRNext for initial extrinsic estimates per camera, followed by a multi-frame bundle adjustment that refines these estimates using reprojection and prior terms to ensure global consistency across the entire camera setup.

Result: The method shows significant improvements in calibration accuracy, achieving a translation error of 0.89 cm and rotation error of 0.038 cm on the KITTI dataset, while reducing translation error from 108.6 cm to 3.1 cm on the Walkley dataset due to enhanced inter-camera consistency.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses the limitations of independent predictions in multi-camera scenarios, demonstrating the value of incorporating geometric constraints for more reliable calibration results.

Abstract: Most learning-based camera-LiDAR calibration methods treat each camera-LiDAR pair independently, ignoring the rigid geometric coupling in multi-camera platforms. As a result, per-camera estimates may be individually accurate yet inconsistent at the system level. We present a two-stage framework for joint multi-camera LiDAR extrinsic calibration that combines learned pairwise matching with geometric refinement. First, CMRNext is applied independently to each camera to produce initial extrinsic estimates and dense 2D-3D correspondences. These predictions are then jointly refined through a multi-frame bundle adjustment with reprojection, per-camera prior, and relative-pose prior terms. This approach converts pairwise predictions into a globally consistent multi-camera calibration. Experiments on KITTI (in-domain for CMRNext) and Walkley (out-of-domain) datasets show improved per-camera accuracy and inter-camera consistency. On KITTI, the method achieves 0.89 cm translation error and 0.038 rotation error. On Walkley, it reduces translation error from 108.6 cm to 3.1 cm, highlighting the benefit of explicit multi-camera coupling when single-camera predictions are less reliable.

</details>


### [110] [SurGe: Improved Surface Geometry in Point Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31577)
*Karim Knaebel,Gonzalo Martin Garcia,Christian Schmidt,Ilya Fradlin,Lucas Nunes,Daan de Geus,Bastian Leibe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents SurGe, a new 3D reconstruction method that enhances local surface geometry accuracy through a point map normal metric, a point gradient matching loss, and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D reconstruction methods struggle with local surface geometry despite good global geometry predictions, necessitating more explicit evaluation of local inaccuracies.

Method: The paper introduces a point map normal metric and employs a point gradient matching loss along with a Neighborhood Attention Decoder for improved feature extraction and upsampling.

Result: SurGe outperforms prior methods in global point map AbsRel and significantly enhances local point map evaluations across various benchmarks.

Conclusion: By emphasizing local geometry through innovative loss functions and attention mechanisms, SurGe demonstrates superior performance in 3D reconstruction tasks.

Abstract: Recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods predict point maps and estimate global 3D geometry remarkably well. However, their predictions still exhibit inaccurate local surface geometry, which is clearly visible qualitatively but only weakly reflected in common metrics. To make these errors more explicit in evaluation, we introduce a point map normal metric that evaluates the local surface orientation induced by neighboring 3D predictions. To reduce these errors, we propose two complementary components: a point gradient matching loss that supervises depth-normalized 3D finite differences, and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and uses Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing. Across eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, our model, SurGe, achieves the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal evaluations.

</details>


### [111] [Recognizing Co-Speech Gestures in-the-Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31589)
*Sindhu B Hegde,K R Prajwal,Andrew Zisserman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Gesture Recognition in the Wild (GRW) dataset to improve multimodal models' ability to understand co-speech gestures linked to specific words, addressing the issue of limited annotated training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the understanding of semantic co-speech gestures in multimodal models, which are currently hindered by insufficient annotated training data.

Method: Introduction of the GRW dataset, featuring 156,688 annotated video clips for mapping gestures to words, and training video models to classify, recognize, and localize gestures.

Result: The GRW dataset enables training for three tasks: classifying gestures as semantic, recognizing corresponding words, and localizing gestures temporally, establishing benchmarks for evaluation.

Conclusion: The GRW dataset represents a significant advancement in the study of co-speech gestures and supports improved performance in multimodal gesture recognition tasks.

Abstract: While humans naturally gesture during speech, only a sparse subset of these movements are visually depictive and semantically linked to specific spoken words. Current multimodal models struggle to capture these semantic co-speech gestures, heavily bottlenecked by a lack of precisely annotated training data. To address this, we introduce the Gesture Recognition in the Wild (GRW) dataset, the first large-scale benchmark designed to map unconstrained human gestures to specific words with frame-accurate temporal boundaries. Comprising 156,688 manually annotated video clips, GRW spans a highly diverse 150-word taxonomy of physical actions, spatial descriptors, and abstract concepts. We leverage GRW to train video models to (a) classify gestures as semantic or not, (b) recognize the word corresponding to a co-speech gesture, and (c) temporally localize the gesture. We also use GRW to establish benchmarks for these three tasks.

</details>


### [112] [TunerDiT: Training-free Progressive Steering of Diffusion Transformer for Multi-Event Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31590)
*Ruotong Liao,Guowen Huang,Qing Cheng,Guangyao Zhai,Lei Zhang,Xun Xiao,Thomas Seidl,Daniel Cremers,Volker Tresp*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents TunerDiT, a method for text-to-video generation that improves handling of long videos with multiple events by utilizing video diffusion transformers and a progressive steering technique.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of generating complex videos with multiple events by leveraging video diffusion transformers and enhancing text-to-video alignment.

Method: TunerDiT introduces two methods: Event-Partitioned Masking to delineate event boundaries while enabling transitions, and Cross-Event Prompt Fusion for refining semantics during generation, without requiring additional training.

Result: TunerDiT achieves state-of-the-art performance on 8 metrics, demonstrating improved text alignment that scales with the number of events.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that TunerDiT effectively balances video consistency and event separation, paving the way for advanced multi-event video generation.

Abstract: Text-to-video (T2V) generation faces challenging questions when generating videos with long horizons containing multiple events. Inspired by the intrinsics of the diffusion process, we probe video diffusion transformers (DiTs) and uncover intrinsic turning points in the DiT denoising trajectory where conditioning text affects generation from global layout to fine-grained details. Building on this finding, we present TunerDiT, a simple yet effective progressive steering method that requires no additional training for multi-event generation. TunerDiT comprises two steering handles: (1) Event-Partitioned Masking that enforces event boundaries while allowing cross-event transition bands; (2) Cross-Event Prompt Fusion that injects neighboring event semantics for late-stage refinement. We contribute a self-curated prompt suite for benchmarking multi-event generation, i.e., Meve. TunerDiT achieves state-of-the-art performance across 8 metrics and offers a tunable trade-off between video consistency and event separation, compared with other training-free methods. The improvement in text alignment increases with the event count, indicating a scaling possibility with increasing event count.

</details>


### [113] [Learning Global Motion with Compact Gaussians for Feed-Forward 4D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31595)
*Mungyeom Kim,Minkyeong Jeon,Honggyu An,Jaewoo Jung,Hyuna Ko,Jisang Han,Hyeonseo Yu,Donghwan Shin,Sunghwan Hong,Takuya Narihira,Kazumi Fukuda,Yuki Mitsufuji,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: C4G is a novel framework for dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video using timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian tokens, addressing issues of existing methods by enabling coherent motion modeling and improved rendering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to improve dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video due to limitations in existing feed-forward methods, particularly issues with duplicated Gaussians and view-dependent biases.

Method: C4G employs compact, timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian query tokens for 4D reconstruction, aggregating features across temporal context to decode 3D Gaussians without per-scene optimization.

Result: C4G demonstrates enhanced novel-view synthesis performance, uses fewer Gaussians, avoids dependency on camera poses, and shows better motion modeling across large temporal gaps.

Conclusion: C4G is a promising approach for dynamic scene reconstruction, improving feature aggregation and supporting tasks like point tracking and dynamic scene understanding.

Abstract: Dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. Existing feed-forward methods predict 3D Gaussians pixel-wise for each frame, suffering from duplicated Gaussians and view-dependent biases that hinder effective learning of scene motion. We present C4G, a feed-forward 4D reconstruction framework built upon a compact set of timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian query tokens. Each token aggregates corresponding features across the full temporal context and decodes a 3D Gaussian whose position is modulated by the target timestamp, enabling globally coherent motion modeling without per-scene optimization. To capture fine-grained details, we further introduce a video diffusion model-based rendering enhancement module. Since our framework effectively aggregates features into Gaussians, we extend this capability to feature lifting, producing a 4D feature field that supports point tracking and dynamic scene understanding. C4G achieves strong novel-view synthesis performance using significantly fewer Gaussians and without requiring camera poses, while exhibiting stronger motion modeling and robustness to large temporal gaps.

</details>


### [114] [CoFiDA-M: Concept-Aware Feature Modulation for Cross-Domain Adaptation with Image-Only Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31591)
*Nurjahan Sultana,Moi Hoon Yap,Xinqi Fan,Wenqi Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents CoFiDA-M, a framework for improving AI skin cancer screening performance by leveraging semantic information during training but deploying solely as an image-only model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a significant drop in performance when AI models for skin cancer detection are applied to consumer-grade images, which hampers their practical use.

Method: The proposed method employs a privileged information framework using MONET concept probabilities to inform a teacher network, which trains a lightweight student model to replicate an edited feature representation.

Result: The image-only student model outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on a multi-dataset benchmark, particularly excelling in melanoma recall.

Conclusion: This approach enables the effective use of noisy metadata, ensuring robust performance across datasets and enhancing the potential for deployment in real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Models for AI-based skin cancer screening suffer a severe performance drop when shifting from expert dermoscopic (source) images to consumer-grade clinical (target) images, hindering real-world deployment. Existing domain adaptation methods often ignore crucial semantic invariants, such as clinical concepts. While new foundation models like MONET can provide this semantic information as dense, probabilistic scores, this metadata is unavailable at test time, creating a deployment paradox for practical image-only screening tools. We address this gap by proposing CoFiDA-M, a privileged information framework that learns from concepts at training time but deploys as an image-only model. Our method trains a teacher network that uses MONET concept probabilities to guide a FiLM modulator, transforming visual features into a semantically ``edited" feature space. A lightweight, image-only student is then trained to reproduce this edited representation, not just the teacher's final predictions. This distillation ``bakes" the clinical reasoning into the student's weights. On a challenging multi-dataset benchmark, our image-only student significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, especially in melanoma recall. Our work provides a practical and generalizable framework for leveraging noisy, probabilistic metadata as privileged information, demonstrating strong cross-dataset robustness and potential for real-world deployment beyond dermatology. Implementation code is available at: https://github.com/mmu-dermatology-research/CoFiDA.git

</details>


### [115] [KLIP: localized distribution shift detection via KL-divergence with diffusion priors in Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31596)
*Alireza Kheirandish,Jihoon Hong,Sara Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel out-of-distribution (OOD) detection metric using Kullback-Leibler divergence for diffusion models, which can effectively identify both entire images and localized patches without requiring prior distribution knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OOD detection methods are often limited by their requirement of knowledge about the shifted distribution and their inability to detect localized shifts, posing challenges for computational imaging applications that rely on inverse problems.

Method: The proposed metric utilizes Kullback-Leibler divergence to compare the diffusion prior and posterior distributions, allowing for OOD detection without the need for calibration data or prior distribution knowledge.

Result: The metric successfully identifies subtle distribution shifts (e.g., between healthy and tumor-affected liver CT scans) and generalizes well across various diffusion models and datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed OOD detection approach enhances the capability to address challenges in real-world imaging scenarios and can effectively localize OOD regions, advancing the applicability of diffusion models in computational imaging.

Abstract: Diffusion models have shown promising performance as data-driven priors for computational imaging, as well as some capacity to detect out-of-distribution (OOD) images. However, existing approaches to OOD detection often require some knowledge of the shifted distribution, fail to detect subtle or localized distribution shifts, and operate on full images, rather than the indirect measurements available in inverse problems. We propose an OOD detection metric based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the diffusion prior and the posterior distribution, that (i) does not require any calibration data or knowledge of the shifted distribution, and (ii) can detect whole images as OOD as well as localize OOD patches within an image. Experimentally, we show that this metric can detect subtle yet semantically meaningful distribution shifts, such as the shift from healthy liver CT scans to those with tumors, and generalizes across different types of diffusion models, datasets, and inverse problems. Our code can be found at https://github.com/voilalab/KLIP.

</details>


### [116] [SOCO: Benchmarking Semantic Object Correspondence in Vision Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31597)
*Olaf Dünkel,Basavaraj Sunagad,Haoran Wang,David T. Hoffmann,Christian Theobalt,Adam Kortylewski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SOCO is introduced as a benchmark for evaluating semantic object correspondence in vision foundation models, offering a consistent methodology and annotations to improve part-level understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in evaluating structured object understanding due to inconsistent protocols and limited supervision in vision foundation models.

Method: SOCO provides a taxonomy of correspondence types, keypoint annotations across 100 categories, and language descriptions to evaluate semantic correspondence.

Result: Experiments show that while vision foundation models encode semantic structure, they struggle with transferring correspondences and fine-grained part localization; LVLMs perform better with text prompts but not in visual reference tasks, highlighting a performance gap.

Conclusion: SOCO serves as a crucial benchmark for assessing structured representation quality in vision and multimodal models, indicating that correspondence performance is a better predictor of success in downstream tasks compared to standard classification metrics.

Abstract: Measuring structured object understanding in vision foundation models remains challenging due to inconsistent evaluation protocols and limited part-level supervision. Semantic correspondence (SC) evaluates this capability by testing whether object parts can be matched across instances and categories under large variations in appearance, viewpoint, and geometry. To enable a systematic SC evaluation, we introduce SOCO, a new benchmark for Semantic Object Correspondence that introduces a taxonomy of correspondence types and provides consistent, functionally meaningful keypoint annotations across 100 categories and over 1M correspondence pairs. In addition, SOCO includes keypoint language descriptions, enabling the evaluation of large vision-language models (LVLMs) and their fine-grained part-level understanding. Comprehensive experiments reveal that (i) vision foundation backbones encode strong semantic structure but transfer correspondences poorly across related categories and only partially capture object-part position, (ii) LVLMs are stronger at text-prompted part localization than at visual-reference cross-image matching, exposing a gap between language-grounded localization and fine-grained visual correspondence, and (iii) correspondence performance predicts performance on dense downstream tasks, including segmentation, tracking, 3D pose estimation, and 3D detection, more strongly than ImageNet classification. Together, these findings position SOCO as a benchmark for structured, part-level representation quality in vision and multimodal foundation models.

</details>


### [117] [Lumos-Nexus: Efficient Frequency Bridging with Homogeneous Latent Space for Video Unified Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31603)
*Jiazheng Xing,Hangjie Yuan,Lingling Cai,Xinyu Liu,Yujie Wei,Fei Du,Hai Ci,Tao Feng,Jiasheng Tang,Weihua Chen,Fan Wang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lumos-Nexus is a training-efficient video generation framework that enhances visual fidelity and reasoning-driven generation by using a lightweight generator for training, followed by a high-capacity pretrained generator during inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high computational cost of integrating a large generator in unified video generation models, thus improving visual quality and reasoning capabilities.

Method: Lumos-Nexus employs a two-stage approach: a lightweight generator for training aligned with a reasoning block, followed by Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB) for inference to transition to a high-capacity pretrained generator.

Result: Lumos-Nexus shows substantial improvements in visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench and performs well in reasoning-driven video generation on VR-Bench.

Conclusion: The proposed framework not only enhances visual fidelity but also maintains strong reasoning-driven performance, filling the gap in existing benchmarks.

Abstract: Connector-based video unified models have demonstrated strong capability in instruction-grounded video synthesis, but integrating a large high-fidelity generator into the unified training loop is computationally prohibitive, limiting achievable visual quality. We therefore propose Lumos-Nexus, a training-efficient unified video generation framework that facilitates the development of strong reasoning-driven generation capabilities while significantly enhancing visual fidelity. Lumos-Nexus adopts a two-stage design: 1) During training, only a lightweight generator is aligned with the understanding block to learn to take in reasoning-driven semantic control. 2) During inference, we introduce Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB) to progressively hand off generation to a high-capacity pretrained generator in the shared latent space, enabling coarse-to-fine refinement and producing high-fidelity videos without compromising reasoning quality. To fill the gap in reasoning-driven video generation benchmarks, we introduce VR-Bench, which assesses a model's capability to translate inferred intent into coherent and semantically aligned video content. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Lumos-Nexus achieves substantial gains in visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench, while exhibiting strong reasoning-based generative performance on VR-Bench. Code and models are available at https://jiazheng-xing.github.io/nexus-lumos-home/.

</details>


### [118] [Linear Scaling Video VLMs for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31598)
*Cristobal Eyzaguirre,Jiajun Wu,Juan Carlos Niebles*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StateKV is a method for improving the efficiency of video vision-language models by enabling linear-time video processing while maintaining accuracy, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational inefficiencies of spatiotemporal self-attention in video encoders, which can grow quadratically with the number of frames, especially in long-horizon settings.

Method: StateKV adapts pretrained long-video VLMs to linear-time processing by utilizing a fixed-capacity, importance-based recurrent state for cross-frame context and maintaining a full per-frame cache for decoding.

Result: StateKV performs closely to full self-attention across multiple long-video benchmarks and significantly outperforms other streaming approximations such as sliding-window techniques, while not requiring fine-tuning or changes to architecture.

Conclusion: StateKV provides a feasible approach towards achieving scalable long-video understanding by reducing computational costs and enhancing model performance without compromising accuracy.

Abstract: Video vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used in long-horizon and streaming settings, yet most video encoders still rely on spatiotemporal self-attention, causing compute and latency to grow quadratically with the number of frames. Existing efficiency methods improve scalability but often lose accuracy relative to full self-attention, for example through aggressive frame/token dropping or coarse attention approximations. We introduce StateKV, an inference-time method that adapts pretrained long-video VLMs to linear-time video prefill by carrying cross-frame context in a fixed-capacity, importance-based recurrent state, paired with a second full per-frame cache used for decoding. Across three long-video benchmarks and seven models spanning three families and multiple scales, StateKV remains close to full self-attention and consistently outperforms dominant sliding-window / recency-based streaming approximations, without fine-tuning or architectural changes. StateKV also reduces video-prefill cost measured FLOPs, enabling stronger accuracy at a fixed compute budget by running larger models. These results suggest a practical step toward scalable long-video understanding.

</details>


### [119] [Representation Forcing for Bottleneck-Free Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31604)
*Yuqing Wang,Zhijie Lin,Ceyuan Yang,Yang Zhao,Fei Xiao,Hao He,Qi Zhao,Zihan Ding,Fuyun Wang,Shuai Wang,Youliang Zhang,Haoqi Fan,Xihui Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Representation Forcing (RF) to improve unified multimodal models (UMMs) by eliminating the reliance on a pretrained VAE, allowing native representation prediction and enhancing both image generation and understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UMMs are limited by the need for a frozen, separately pretrained VAE for image generation, creating a structural bottleneck.

Method: The paper proposes Representation Forcing (RF), which allows the decoder to predict visual representations as intermediate tokens before generating pixels, facilitating better integration within the model's architecture.

Result: The pixel-space model incorporating RF matches the performance of state-of-the-art VAE-based unified models in image generation and generally outperforms its VAE-based counterpart in image understanding.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate a significant advancement toward developing end-to-end UMMs without structural bottlenecks, benefiting both perception and generation.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) aim to handle perception and generation in a single model. Yet existing UMMs still rely on a frozen, separately pretrained VAE for image generation, imposing a structural bottleneck. Naively removing it introduces a quality gap, as the model must learn both high-level structure and low-level details from raw pixels. In this paper, we propose Representation Forcing (RF), a technique that closes this gap by making representation prediction a native capability of the model. Concretely, RF forces the decoder to autoregressively predict visual representations as intermediate tokens before pixels; these tokens then stay in context to guide pixel diffusion within the same backbone. By turning representations from perception outputs into generation targets, RF eliminates the need for any external generative latent space. We find that RF benefits both understanding and generation. On image generation, our pixel-space model with RF matches state-of-the-art VAE-based unified models. On image understanding, pixel-space RF generally outperforms its VAE-based variant. Together, these results offer an effective step toward end-to-end, bottleneck-free UMMs.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [120] [Protocol for evaluating ChatGPT in biomedical association generation and verification using a RAG-enabled, cross-model majority voting workflow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30400)
*Ahmed Abdeen Hamed,Luis M. Rocha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a protocol to assess ChatGPT's effectiveness in generating biomedical associations, emphasizing validation through ontologies and literature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the generative capabilities and reliability of ChatGPT in creating disease-related biomedical connections.

Method: The protocol involves generating associations, validating them using biomedical ontologies, and verifying through literature. It includes a strategy for self-consistency and employs Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to enhance semantic verification.

Result: The proposed approach allows for accurate validation of biomedical associations, revealing the generative reliability of ChatGPT and identifying hallucinated outputs.
