*Yuri Balashov,Rex VanHorn,Mingxi Xu,Austin Downes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper develops methods for freelance translators to evaluate translation technologies, focusing on offline translation for confidentiality-sensitive domains. It benchmarks local language models using an expanded multilingual corpus and finds that some local models perform comparably to commercial systems, highlighting their utility for privacy-constrained environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for reliable offline translation technologies for confidentiality-sensitive domains, where cloud-based solutions are not viable due to privacy constraints.

Method: Expanded the Reeve Foundation Trilingual Corpus (RFTC) into a multilingual corpus (RFMC) by adding German and Simplified Chinese translations; benchmarked local language models using consistent single-prompt calls on a set of 1000+ sentences without fine-tuning.

Result: Local LLMs demonstrated significant performance variation by language direction and model size, with top local models performing on par with local NMT systems and a frontier LLM, though trailing behind the best commercial NMTs.

Conclusion: The results highlight the potential of local LLMs for privacy-sensitive translation needs and offer insights for future research on enhancing model performance and multilingual support.

Abstract: Building on our previous work, this paper develops practical, low-barrier methods for freelance translators and smaller language service providers to evaluate translation technologies using rigorous yet accessible analytic methods. Here we address a high-stakes, specialized need: offline translation for confidentiality-sensitive domains in which privacy constraints preclude the use of cloud-based engines and commercial LLMs. We expand the Reeve Foundation Trilingual Corpus (RFTC) used in our previous work into a multilingual corpus (RFMC) by adding sentence-aligned German and Simplified Chinese reference translations. We then benchmark several locally runnable language models (via Ollama) across four language directions on 1000+ sentences selected from this corpus. We use consistent single-prompt calls without fine-tuning or domain adaptation, comparing local LLM outputs against commercial NMTs (DeepL, Baidu), a frontier LLM (GPT-5.2), and professional-grade local NMT systems (OPUS-CAT, NeuralDesktop, Promt). Automatic evaluation is conducted with MATEO. Results reveal substantial variation in local LLM performance across language directions and model sizes. The best local LLMs match or surpass local NMT systems and a frontier LLM, though they remain behind top commercial NMTs. These findings underscore the viability of carefully selected local LLM translation for privacy-constrained professionals and inform future research on model scaling and multilingual capability.

</details>


### [213] [Scaling Conversational Hungarian ASR: The BEA-Dialogue+ Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31469)
*Máté Gedeon,Piroska Zsófia Barta,Péter Mihajlik,Katalin Mády*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents BEA-Dialogue+, an expanded Hungarian dialogue corpus that offers 200 hours of data compared to the original BEA-Dialogue's 85 hours, allowing for a controlled study of training data trade-offs in automatic speech recognition systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The limited availability of dialogue-style training data for Hungarian ASR systems necessitated the development of a larger corpus to improve model performance.

Method: The authors expanded the original BEA-Dialogue corpus by relaxing the disjoint speaker condition, providing 200 hours of transcribed conversations; they evaluated various ASR models and SOT-based fine-tuning on both corpus versions.

Result: The evaluations indicated that the larger BEA-Dialogue+ corpus posed challenges for models lacking fine-tuning, while SOT-based adaptation led to significant improvements in various error rates.

Conclusion: BEA-Dialogue+ serves as a more extensive and rigorous benchmark for Hungarian dialogue ASR, offering a valuable resource for developing and assessing dialogue transcription systems.

Abstract: Conversational automatic speech recognition in Hungarian is constrained by the limited amount of publicly available dialogue-style training data. The BEA-Dialogue corpus addresses this need, but its strictly speaker-disjoint train/dev/eval split reduces the usable material to only 85 hours. In this paper, we introduce BEA-Dialogue+, an expanded version of the corpus that relaxes the split criterion for experimenters and dialogue partners while preserving complete separation of the primary speakers. This results in 200 hours of transcribed natural conversations and enables a controlled study of the trade-off between additional training data and speaker overlap across the splits. We evaluate several Whisper- and FastConformer-based models on both corpus versions, including Serialized Output Training (SOT)-based fine-tuning for dialogue transcription. Our results show that the larger corpus is more challenging for models without fine-tuning, whereas SOT-based adaptation yields consistent improvements in WER, CER, cpWER, and cpCER. Overall, BEA-Dialogue+ provides a substantially larger yet still demanding benchmark for Hungarian dialogue ASR, and a practical resource for training and evaluating dialogue transcription systems.

</details>


### [214] [Language Models Can Resolve Reference Compositionally, But It's Not Their Native Strength: The Case of the Personal Relation Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31480)
*Bart Evelo,Meaghan Fowlie,Denis Paperno*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study investigates the compositional abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in natural language understanding, comparing their performance on Extensional and Intensional semantic tasks against human capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether LLMs truly possess compositional interpretation abilities in natural language, focusing on both Extensional (referential) and Intensional (sense representation) tasks.

Method: Evaluation of LLMs and humans using the Personal Relation Task, where participants interpret noun phrases in a context involving relationships between people.

Result: Humans outperform LLMs on Extensional tasks, while LLMs do better on Intensional tasks, highlighting a contrast in strengths between the two.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that LLMs lack referential grounding, a key aspect that hinders their ability to replicate human-like language understanding.

Abstract: Do neural models, such as Large Language Models, genuinely acquire compositional abilities for interpretation of natural language? When we talk about semantic interpretation, we can distinguish two complementary aspects: establishing what an expression refers to in the world (which we call the Extensional task) and representing its sense in a structured way (which we call the Intensional task). We evaluate LLMs and humans on both tasks in the setting of the Personal Relation Task (Paperno 2022) in which, given a universe of people and their relationships with each other, one is asked to interpret a noun phrase such as "Amber's parent's friend". Here, for the Intensional task, the answer is the formula "friend(parent(amber))", and for the Extensional task, the person. We find that humans and LLMs show opposite strengths: humans perform better on Extensional than Intensional tasks, and LLMs vice versa. Our methodology brings greater nuance to the understanding of compositional abilities in modern machine learning models. Our results support the notion that the lack of referential grounding in LLM training is a crucial missing component in mimicking human-like language understanding.

</details>


### [215] [BenHalluEval: A Multi-Task Hallucination Evaluation Framework for Large Language Models on Bengali](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31483)
*Shefayat E Shams Adib,Ahmed Alfey Sani,Ekramul Alam Esham,Ajwad Abrar,Ishmam Tashdeed,Md Taukir Azam Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BenHalluEval introduces a framework for assessing hallucination in LLMs for Bengali, covering four tasks with a new metric, BenHalluScore, that reveals significant variability in hallucination rates across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate hallucination in LLMs for Bengali, a low-resource language with no prior dedicated studies.

Method: Developed BenHalluEval, a framework with 12,000 hallucinated examples across four tasks and evaluated seven LLMs using a dual-track protocol for accuracy in measuring hallucination.

Result: BenHalluScore revealed variation in hallucination rates from 7.72% to 55.42% across models, and chain-of-thought prompting did not uniformly enhance performance.

Conclusion: BenHalluEval is the first hallucination benchmark for Bengali, demonstrating that traditional evaluation methods may not be suitable for low-resource languages.

Abstract: Despite Bengali being the sixth most spoken language in the world, no prior work has systematically evaluated hallucination in large language models (LLMs) for Bengali. We introduce BenHalluEval, a fine-grained hallucination evaluation framework for Bengali covering four tasks: Generative Question Answering (GQA), Bangla-English Code-Mixed QA, Summarization, and Reasoning. We construct 12,000 hallucinated candidates using GPT-5.4 across twelve task-specific hallucination types, drawn from three existing Bengali datasets, and evaluate seven LLMs spanning reasoning-oriented, multilingual, and Bengali-centric categories under a dual-track protocol that independently measures false-positive rate on ground-truth instances (Track A) and hallucination detection rate on hallucinated candidates (Track B). To jointly penalise both failure modes and prevent inflated scores from uniform response bias, we propose BenHalluScore, a dual-track calibration metric that ranges from 7.72% to 55.42% across models and tasks, revealing substantial variation in hallucination calibration. Chain-of-thought prompting, applied as a mitigation strategy, shifts response distributions without consistently improving hallucination discrimination. BenHalluEval establishes the first dedicated hallucination benchmark for Bengali and highlights the inadequacy of single-track and prompting-only evaluation approaches for low-resource language settings. The dataset and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BanglaHalluEval-EB77.

</details>


### [216] [Are Full Rollouts Necessary for On-Policy Distillation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31490)
*Yaocheng Zhang,Jiajun Chai,Songjun Tu,Yuqian Fu,Xiaohan Wang,Wei Lin,Guojun Yin,Qichao Zhang,Yuanheng Zhu,Dongbin Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces two horizon-control strategies for on-policy distillation (OPD) that enhance training efficiency by minimizing reliance on full rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in standard OPD caused by computationally expensive full rollouts and unreliable feedback during late training stages.

Method: The authors propose Progressive OPD (POPD), which gradually increases the rollout horizon, and Truncated OPD (TOPD), which uses reliable truncated rollouts for distillation.

Result: Experiments show that POPD improves training efficiency of OPD by up to 3 times, while TOPD achieves similar performance to OPD using only 10% of the rollout horizon.

Conclusion: Controlling the rollout horizon leads to more efficient OPD, reducing resource consumption without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense teacher feedback along rollouts generated by the student and has emerged as a promising post-training paradigm for long-horizon reasoning. However, standard OPD typically generates full rollouts during training, which is computationally expensive and may expose the student to unreliable teacher feedback at late rollout positions, especially during early training. We identify the rollout horizon as a key bottleneck in OPD that substantially impacts training efficiency. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), OPD does not require a complete trajectory or a final answer reward to provide learning signals. This observation suggests that full rollouts may not always be necessary for effective OPD. Motivated by this insight, we propose two simple horizon-control strategies: Progressive OPD (POPD), which gradually expands the rollout horizon during training, and Truncated OPD (TOPD), which permanently performs distillation on reliable truncated rollouts. Experiments on mathematical reasoning show that POPD improves the training efficiency of OPD by up to 3$\times$, while TOPD matches OPD performance using only 10\% of the rollout horizon, leading to substantial wall-clock and memory reductions. These results demonstrate that controlling the rollout horizon offers a simple and practical path to more efficient OPD.

</details>


### [217] [Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations for LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31494)
*Zheyu Zhang,Shuo Yang,Gjergji Kasneci*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CoRP, a gradient-free method that consolidates multiple rewarded language model perturbations into a single deployable model, enhancing performance while reducing inference costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve inference efficiency and extend the capabilities of language models by eliminating the need for multiple forward passes required in traditional ensemble methods.

Method: CoRP combines rewarded perturbations using techniques such as reward-weighted aggregation and compatibility-aware reweighting, employing a held-out validation gate without passing gradients through the pretrained model.

Result: CoRP demonstrates an average improvement of 8.1 points across five language models and tasks, surpassing the performance of single-inference RandOpt and recovering significant gains from ensemble predictions.

Conclusion: CoRP provides a viable solution to enhance language model performance and efficiency, effectively consolidating rewarded outputs into a single model update.

Abstract: Post-training of language models is commonly framed as a sample-score-update loop implemented by gradient descent. A recent line of work, exemplified by RandOpt, relocates this loop to weight space, sampling Gaussian perturbations around a pretrained model and ensembling the top-K rewarded specialists at inference. While competitive with PPO and GRPO under matched training compute, this prediction-level ensemble incurs K forward passes per test example and does not extend cleanly to free-form generation. We ask whether the rewarded population can instead be folded into a single deployable model, replacing the inference-time ensemble with one consolidated update. A split-half analysis over 25 model-task pairs reveals reproducible low-rank structure in every case. We turn this geometry into CoRP (Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations), a gradient-free operator that combines reward-weighted aggregation, compatibility-aware reweighting, and a held-out validation gate, with no gradient flowing through the language model. Across five language models from 0.5B to 8B and five tasks covering math, code, and creative writing, CoRP improves the base model by 8.1 points on average. Using one tenth of RandOpt's perturbation budget, CoRP exceeds single-inference RandOpt by 6.5 points and recovers more than half of the gain of the 50-pass majority-vote ensemble, at one forward pass per test example.

</details>


### [218] [Reliable Multilingual Orthopedic Decision Support from Clinical Narratives: Language-Aware Adaptation and Verification-Guided Deferral](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31512)
*Danish Ali,Li Xiaojian,Sundas Iqbal,Farrukh Zaidi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of multilingual orthopedic decision support in low-resource healthcare contexts by proposing a framework that evaluates various transformer encoders for classifying orthopedic notes in English, Hindi, and Punjabi. It highlights the performance of IndicBERT-HPA, which improves multilingual representation and reliability in clinical predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve orthopedic decision support in low-resource settings where linguistic and terminological challenges hinder effective healthcare delivery.

Method: The method involves analyzing the performance of multilingual transformer encoders, including a fine-tuned DistilBERT baseline and IndicBERT-HPA, with a focus on various performance metrics beyond simple accuracy.

Result: IndicBERT-HPA outperforms other models, achieving an averaged Macro-F1 of 0.8792, Macro-AUROC of 0.894, and AUPRC of 0.902 under natural prevalence conditions.

Conclusion: The findings demonstrate that the proposed framework enhances multilingual clinical decision support by integrating a selective-verification layer, ensuring reliability and explicit deferral in predictions.

Abstract: Multilingual orthopedic decision support remains challenging in low-resource healthcare settings, where clinical narratives contain specialized terminology, mixed scripts, incomplete evidence, label imbalance and language-dependent documentation patterns. This article presents a reliability-oriented framework for classifying free-text orthopedic notes in English, Hindi and Punjabi. We compare task-aligned multilingual transformer encoders, a task-fine-tuned DistilBERT baseline, zero-shot instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) and a domain-adaptive encoder, IndicBERT-HPA. IndicBERT-HPA augments IndicBERT with language-aware orthopedic adapter heads to support clinically relevant multilingual representation learning. Evaluation extends beyond aggregate accuracy to per-class performance, ROC-AUC, AUPRC, expected calibration error, cross-language stability and robustness under controlled balanced and natural-prevalence distributions. The evaluated zero-shot LLMs remain substantially less effective than task-adapted encoders for closed-set classification, with language-dependent instability. Under natural clinical prevalence, IndicBERT-HPA achieves the strongest overall performance, reaching an averaged Macro-F1 of 0.8792, Macro-AUROC of 0.894 and AUPRC of 0.902. We further implement a deterministic selective-verification layer combining confidence gating, evidence-consistency checking and language-risk screening. On a randomly selected held-out 5,000-record subset, it achieves 84.4% selective accuracy and 0.76 selective Macro-F1 at 72.3% coverage, compared with 71.5% accuracy and 0.65 Macro-F1 for accept-all prediction. These results support reliability-oriented multilingual clinical decision support with explicit deferral.

</details>


### [219] [If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31514)
*Adrian de Wynter*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper critiques the assumption of anthropomorphic attributes in LLMs, suggesting that such properties may be substrate-dependent and non-unique. It advocates for empirical measurement criteria rather than generalized claims about LLMs' capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the common attribution of anthropomorphic qualities to LLMs and highlight the empirical limitations in assessing their capabilities.

Method: A simple neural network is trained on the videogame Age of Empires II to illustrate the point that attributes ascribed to LLMs can arise from diverse substrates, not exclusively LLMs themselves.

Result: The study finds that purported anthropomorphic attributes are not unique to LLMs and are dependent on their substrates, leading to circular or uninformative conclusions when generalized.

Conclusion: The paper proposes a 'null' assumption of non-uniqueness for LLMs when designing experiments, emphasizes the need for explicit empirical measurement criteria, and discusses potential objections.

Abstract: Much research has been carried out on large language models (LLMs) and LLM-powered agentic workflows. However, many works within the field state emergence of, ascribe to, or assume, generalised anthropomorphic attributes to them (e.g., morality or understanding of natural language). Our goal is not to argue in favour or against the existence of these attributes, but to point out that these conclusions could be incorrect. For this we build and train a simple neural network on the videogame Age of Empires II, and note that any entity in a sufficiently-powerful substrate, such as LEGO or the Greater Boston Area, could also present such attributes. Hence, the purported anthropomorphic attributes of LLMs are empirically non-unique: although some properties (e.g., responses to prompts) could remain constant, others, such as the interpretation of their perceived behaviour, might change with the substrate. Thus, any empirically-grounded discussion requires explicit measurement criteria; otherwise the interpretation is left to the representation. We then show that assuming that these attributes exist or not in a system, independent of the substrate and in a generalised way, leads to either circular or uninformative conclusions, regardless of the experimenter's viewpoint on the subject. Finally we propose a 'null' assumption, where one assumes LLM non-uniqueness instead of assuming anthropomorphic attributes to set up an experiment, along with examples of it. We also discuss potential objections to our work, briefly survey the field, and prove that \textit{Age of Empires II} is functionally- and Turing-complete.

</details>


### [220] [Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31545)
*Yilun Qiu,Xiaoyan Zhao,Yang Zhang,Yuxin Chen,Cilin Yan,Jiayin Cai,Xiaolong Jiang,Yao Hu,Yoko Yamakata,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PARL, a framework for personalized evaluation of LLMs that formulates the evaluation as a learning problem, addressing the need for effective assessment of user-centric model behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs transition to user-centric agents, traditional evaluation methods fail to assess subjective user preferences; thus, personalized evaluation is crucial.

Method: PARL employs a learning-based approach, utilizing user interaction histories to create preference-aware evaluation rubrics, validated through a self-validation mechanism and a discriminative reinforcement learning objective.

Result: Experiments demonstrate PARL's ability to induce high-fidelity rubrics that identify user-aligned responses, generalizing effectively across various users and tasks.

Conclusion: PARL enhances personalized evaluation by capturing stable user preferences and evaluative patterns, contributing to better alignment of LLMs with individual user needs.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve from general-purpose assistants to user-centric agents, personalization has become central to aligning model behavior with individual preferences, making the evaluation of personalized alignment a critical bottleneck. Existing evaluation methods-ranging from automatic metrics to LLM-as-a-judge approaches-fail to capture subjective, user-specific preferences embedded in long-term interaction histories. We identify three essential principles for reliable and effective personalized evaluation: Representativeness, User-Consistency, and Discriminativeness. To address these principles, we introduce Personalized Evaluation as Learning, a paradigm that formulates personalized evaluation as a learning problem rather than a static judgment. Under this paradigm, we propose PARL (Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation), a framework that learns to induce preference-aware evaluation rubrics directly from raw user histories and performs a self-validation mechanism to ensure consistency with the user's preferences. PARL integrates rubric induction with a discriminative reinforcement learning objective that contrasts user-authored responses against competitive personalized model outputs, enabling the learned rubrics to capture precise, user-specific decision boundaries. Experiments on real-world personalized text generation tasks show that PARL consistently induces high-fidelity rubrics that reliably identify user-aligned responses and generalize across users and tasks, while capturing stable stylistic preferences and fine-grained evaluative patterns. To ensure reproducibility, our code is available at https://github.com/SnowCharmQ/PARL.

</details>


### [221] [Semantic Triplet Restoration: A Novel Protocol for Hierarchical Table Understanding in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31550)
*Yibin Zhao,Fangxin Shang,Dingrui Yang,Yuqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents STR, a method for table question answering that simplifies semantic relations by rewriting table cells into atomic facts, enhancing efficiency over current HTML or Markdown representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve table question answering by eliminating the inefficiencies of HTML or Markdown while addressing semantic relations in complex table layouts.

Method: Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR) rewrites cell content into atomic facts consisting of item paths, feature paths, and values, enabling a more straightforward semantic representation. TripletQL serves as a router for selecting relevant triplet subsets for queries.

Result: STR outperforms or matches HTML-based methods across multiple benchmarks and reduces token usage, especially benefiting smaller models and extensive table contexts.

Conclusion: Explicit semantic representations through STR enhance the performance and efficiency of table question answering systems, making them particularly valuable for models with constrained inference capabilities.

Abstract: Table question answering requires models to recover semantic relations encoded implicitly by two-dimensional layout, merged cells, and hierarchical headers. Current pipelines typically use HTML or Markdown as intermediate table representations, but these layout-oriented serializations introduce markup overhead and require large language models to infer header-cell alignments from row and column spans. We propose Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR), a protocol that rewrites each cell as an atomic fact <item path, feature path, value>, where the item path specifies the row-wise entity, the feature path specifies the hierarchical attribute, and the value contains the cell content. We also present TripletQL, a lightweight query-aware router that uses STR to select an appropriate rendering or filtered subset of triplets for each question. Across four Chinese and English table-QA benchmarks, STR matches or improves upon HTML-based baselines while reducing input tokens. The relative benefit grows for smaller language models and longer table contexts, suggesting that explicit semantic representations are especially useful under constrained inference budgets. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Phoenix-ni/STR.git .

</details>


### [222] [What Am I Missing? Question-Answering as Hidden State Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31561)
*Chu Fei Luo,Samuel Dahan,Xiaodan Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes using question-asking as an intervention to improve reasoning in LLMs by analyzing hidden states, presenting a student-teacher framework to assess and maximize the correctness likelihood through a gating policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the under-explored mechanisms of test-time reasoning and variability in LLM outputs based on similar inputs.

Method: Implement a student-teacher setting where the student asks the teacher questions, training a probe on the hidden state to predict correctness before receiving answers. Frame question-asking as a sequential decision problem using a gating policy to maximize correctness likelihood.

Result: The hidden state during question generation provides a meaningful signal related to the trajectory's correctness. However, interventions can equally harm correct trajectories as they help recover incorrect ones, indicating challenges in model self-refinement.

Conclusion: The gap between diagnosis and correction suggests limitations in language models' capability to self-refine under uncertainty, highlighting the need for careful application of inquiry-based interventions.

Abstract: Test-time reasoning has become a significant field of study since the introduction of chain-of-thought reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, the mechanisms of this reasoning process are still under-explored -- from the same input prompt, and even the same partial solution, LLMs can produce varied answers if sampled multiple times. We propose to leverage question-asking as an inference-time intervention that articulates information about the model's hidden state. To achieve that, we present a student-teacher setting where a student asks questions to a teacher. We train a probe on the student's hidden state before and after asking a question and find it is predictive of the trajectory's final correctness, even before generating the teacher's answer. This suggests there is a meaningful signal from the self-diagnosis that occurs during question generation rather than information transfer from the teacher. We then frame question-asking as a sequential decision problem, using this probe as a quality score, and define a gating policy to ask questions that maximize likelihood of correctness. We find that the success of question-asking as an intervention is largely dependent on the model's self-consistency. Our empirical results show a gap between detection and recovery; while our gating policy captures model correctness and uncertainty, interventions are equally likely to harm correct trajectories as they are to recover incorrect ones. This gap between diagnosis and correction has broader implications on language models' capacity for self-refinement under uncertainty.

</details>


### [223] [Disagreeing Rationales: Rethinking Classification and Explainability Evaluation in Hate Speech Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31563)
*Benedetta Muscato,Beiduo Chen,Gizem Gezici,Barbara Plank,Fosca Giannotti*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the challenges and methods of evaluating human-generated labels and rationales in NLP tasks, particularly hate speech detection, and proposes a unified framework for systematic analysis of classification and explainability metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the variation in human rationales and improve the evaluation of labeling in subjective NLP tasks.

Method: A unified supervision framework is presented that integrates diverse models and metrics, focusing on predictive and distributional classification properties and explainability dimensions.

Result: The study finds that both hard and soft evaluation metrics favor softer rationale representations, suggesting they are more effective at capturing human reasoning nuances.

Conclusion: The findings indicate a need to rethink evaluation approaches in subjective NLP tasks, emphasizing the importance of soft representations in model performance.

Abstract: Human disagreement is ubiquitous and well-known in labeling. However, variation in explanations, captured through token-level human rationales, remains far less explored. At the same time, it is unclear how to best evaluate human labels and rationales -- or even how to best aggregate rationales beyond majority vote -- in light of this variation. Yet, rationales may provide additional insights into the richness of human reasoning, that may differ in style, values and interpretations -- especially in subjective NLP tasks like hate speech detection. In this work, we unify diverse models, training strategies, loss functions, and existing evaluation metrics under a single protocol by systematically re-implementing them across different label and rationale representation spaces. Classification metrics are organized around two key properties -- predictive and distributional -- while explainability metrics through three complementary dimensions: plausibility, faithfulness, and complexity. In this unified supervision framework, we evaluate model behavior across classification and explainability metrics, as well as metric sensitivity to the choice of label (hard and soft) and rationale representation space (hard, intermediate and soft). Results show that both hard and soft metrics favor softer representations, highlighting their effectiveness in capturing variation and the need to rethink evaluation in subjective NLP.

</details>


### [224] [UniAudio-Token: Empowering Semantic Speech Tokenizers with General Audio Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31521)
*Yuhan Song,Linhao Zhang,Aiwei Liu,Chuhan Wu,Sijun Zhang,Wei Jia,Yuan Liu,Houfeng Wang,Xiao Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: UniAudio-Token enhances semantic speech tokenizers for broader audio perception while maintaining speech capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of semantic speech tokenizers in non-speech audio tasks due to acoustic blindness caused by their linguistic focus.

Method: UniAudio-Token uses Semantic-Acoustic Primitives for structured supervision of audio and employs Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium for adaptive detail restoration.

Result: UniAudio-Token achieves superior universal representations and high-fidelity speech generation, outperforming baseline tokenizers in tasks.

Conclusion: The framework serves as a unified audio interface, effectively integrating with downstream LLMs, and the code and model are publicly available.

Abstract: Semantic speech tokenizers have become a widely used interface for Audio-LLMs, owing to their compact single-codebook design and strong linguistic alignment. However, their focus on linguistic abstraction induces acoustic blindness, limiting their applicability beyond speech-centric tasks. We propose UniAudio-Token, a framework that empowers semantic tokenizers with general audio perception without compromising speech ability. Instead of altering the semantic paradigm, UniAudio-Token mitigates its information loss through two key innovations: (1) Semantic-Acoustic Primitives (SAP) provide structured supervision by decomposing audio into linguistic content, vocal attributes, and auditory-scene primitives; and (2) Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium (SAE) introduces a content-aware gating mechanism that adaptively restores fine-grained acoustic details from shallow layers. Extensive evaluations show that UniAudio-Token learns comprehensive universal representations while preserving high-fidelity speech generation. When integrated with downstream LLMs, it outperforms all single-codebook baseline tokenizers on both understanding and generation tasks, effectively serving as a unified audio interface. We publicly release all our code, including training and inference scripts, together with the model checkpoints at https://github.com/Tencent/Universal_Audio_Tokenizer.

</details>


### [225] [What Gets Unmasked First? Trajectory Analysis of Diffusion Models for Graph-to-Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31564)
*Qing Wang,Jacob Devasier,Chengkai Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study systematically explores masked diffusion language models for graph-to-text tasks, revealing unique generation patterns and proposing improvements for structural decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how masked diffusion language models differ from autoregressive models in generating text from graphs and to improve their performance.

Method: The study analyzes token generation trajectories in MDLMs, identifies issues with supervised fine-tuning, and proposes lambda-scaled structural decoding as an inference-time modification.

Result: The proposed method improves generation accuracy by 9.4 BLEU-4, and the introduction of Graph-LLaDA shows enhanced generalization over previous baselines on cross-dataset evaluations.

Conclusion: MDLMs exhibit superior entity-first generation and generalize better than autoregressive models, with new strategies proposed to mitigate token anchoring issues.

Abstract: We present the first systematic study of masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) for graph-to-text generation. We analyze MDLM generation trajectories -- the order in which tokens are unmasked during iterative decoding -- and find that, unlike autoregressive LLMs which generate text linearly, MDLMs naturally prioritize entities first, followed by relational and function words, with structural tokens resolved last. We further identify a previously undocumented failure mode of supervised fine-tuning: SFT disrupts this strategy by prematurely anchoring structural sentence-ending tokens early in the decoding trajectory, effectively fixing the output length which can lead to omitted or hallucinated information. To address this, we propose lambda-scaled structural decoding, a training-free inference-time modification that downweights structural token confidence and recovers +9.4 BLEU-4. Finally, we introduce Graph-LLaDA, which integrates a Graph Transformer encoder into LLaDA's decoding process to explicitly incorporate relational graph structure. Cross-dataset evaluation on LAGRANGE reveals that previous baselines overfit to dataset-specific patterns, while LLM- and MDLM-based approaches generalize significantly better.

</details>


### [226] [LongTraceRL: Learning Long-Context Reasoning from Search Agent Trajectories with Rubric Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31584)
*Nianyi Lin,Jiajie Zhang,Lei Hou,Juanzi Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LongTraceRL improves long-context reasoning in large language models by generating challenging training data and implementing a fine-grained rubric reward system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance long-context reasoning capabilities in large language models by tackling issues with distractors and intermediate reasoning supervision.

Method: Introduces LongTraceRL, which constructs challenging multi-hop question datasets using tiered distractors and applies a positive-only rubric reward based on entity-level verification.

Result: LongTraceRL outperforms existing methods on multiple long-context benchmarks and promotes better reasoning quality.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates significant improvements in evidence-based reasoning for large language models.

Abstract: Long-context reasoning remains a central challenge for large language models, which often fail to locate and integrate key information in extensive distracting content. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has shown promise for this task, yet existing methods are limited by low-confusability distractors and sparse, outcome-only reward signals that cannot supervise intermediate reasoning steps. To address these issues, we introduce \textsc{LongTraceRL}. For data construction, we generate multi-hop questions via knowledge graph random walks and leverage search agent trajectories to build \emph{tiered distractors}: documents the agent read but did not cite (high confusability) and documents that appeared in search results but were never opened (low confusability), producing training contexts that are far more challenging than those built by random sampling or one-shot search. For reward design, we propose a \emph{rubric reward} that uses the gold entities along each reasoning chain as fine-grained, entity-level process supervision. This rubric reward is applied only to responses with correct final answers (positive-only strategy), distinguishing the reasoning quality among correct responses and preventing reward hacking. Experiments on three reasoning LLMs (4B--30B) across five long-context benchmarks demonstrate that \textsc{LongTraceRL} consistently outperforms strong baselines and encourages comprehensive, evidence-grounded reasoning. Codes, datasets and models are available at \href{https://github.com/THU-KEG/LongTraceRL}{https://github.com/THU-KEG/LongTraceRL}.

</details>


### [227] [Language Models Learn Constructional Semantics, Not To Mention Syntax: Investigating LM Understanding of Paired-Focus Constructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31586)
*Wesley Scivetti,Ethan Wilcox,Nathan Schneider,Kanishka Misra,Leonie Weissweiler*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Modestly sized open-source models can understand rare Paired-Focus constructions, with semantic understanding emerging later in training, showing a linkage to general world knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate if open-source models can robustly understand rare constructions in language and to explore the learning dynamics involved in acquiring this knowledge.

Method: A novel dataset was constructed to test meanings of rare Paired-Focus constructions using models of varying sizes, architectures, and pretraining datasets.

Result: Several modestly sized models demonstrated sensitivity to the forms and meanings of these constructions, though larger models trained on human-scale data failed in meaning evaluations.

Conclusion: Modestly sized open-source models can effectively grasp rare Paired-Focus constructions, indicating a relationship between construction knowledge and general meaning understanding.

Abstract: Grasping the semantics of rare constructions (form-meaning pairings) has been shown to be a challenging problem that has currently only been solved by the largest LLMs. It remains an open question if open-source models have robust constructional understanding, and if so, what learning dynamics underlie the acquisition of this knowledge. Focusing on a set of rare Paired-Focus constructions in English (e.g. "let alone", "much less"), we construct a novel dataset to test their meanings using both scalar adjectival semantics and general world knowledge. Testing a wide range of models differing in parameter count, architecture, and pretraining dataset size, we find that several modestly sized models are sensitive to both the forms and the meanings of Paired-Focus constructions, though models trained on human-scale data fail at all meaning evaluations. Turning to training dynamics for a set of open-checkpoint models, we find that Paired-Focus understanding emerges later in training than Paired-Focus syntactic knowledge, and that learning of Paired-Focus semantics is correlated with gains in some domains of world knowledge. Overall, our empirical results support the conclusion that modestly sized open-source models can grasp the rare Paired-Focus constructions, and demonstrate a connection between knowledge of Paired-Focus constructions and other meaning domains.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [228] [Learning-Based Navigation for Indoor Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30468)
*Tri-Tin Nguyen,Tien-Dat Nguyen,Gia-Uy Le,Vinh Nguyen,Vinh-Hao Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots, combining a supervised neural global planner with a refined local planner using reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance navigation capabilities in indoor environments by integrating learning methods for both global and local planning.

Method: The approach uses a combination of a supervised neural global planner trained on A* expert trajectories and a local planner developed via behavior cloning and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).

Result: The method provides feasible global trajectories and reliable local commands for autonomous navigation in simulated and real-world scenarios with obstacles.

Conclusion: The integration of learning-based global planning with policy refinement improves the safety and effectiveness of indoor mobile robot navigation.

Abstract: This paper presents a learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots. The proposed method combines a supervised neural global planner, trained from cost-aware A* expert trajectories, with the proposed Learning-Based DWA local planner, which is formulated as discrete candidate selection over the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) action lattice. For local planning, the policy is first trained by behavior cloning and then refined by Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under feasibility-aware masking. The framework is implemented and evaluated in both simulated and real-world indoor environments. Experimental results show that the proposed method generates feasible global routes and reliable local motion commands for safe goal-directed navigation in the presence of obstacles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating learning-based global planning with reinforcement-learning-refined local control for indoor mobile robot navigation. The source code will be released at https://ntdathp.github.io/rl_robot_web/.

</details>


### [229] [ELAN4D: Embodiment-Centric 4D Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Models via Plug-and-Play Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30484)
*Zeyuan He,Bowen Yang,Zhirui Fang,Keru Zhou,Lei Jiang,Jingjing Qian,Fan Mo,Junchi Yan,Philip Torr,Xiu Li,Li Jiang,Jialin Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ELAN4D is a framework that enhances Vision-Language-Action models for robotic manipulation through future keypoint tracking, improving performance and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models fail to generalize under out-of-distribution perturbations due to reactive action regressing based on current observations, lacking future dynamic modeling.

Method: ELAN4D utilizes an embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training framework with 3D displacement tracks from robot keypoints for predictive spatio-temporal supervision, employing a lightweight track decoder for integration.

Result: ELAN4D outperforms strong VLA baselines in extensive experiments, particularly under variable conditions such as camera and layout shifts.

Conclusion: The framework effectively provides robust and generalizable manipulation policies through embodiment-centric 4D supervision.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise for robotic manipulation, yet most existing policies operate reactively by directly regressing actions from current observations, without explicitly modeling future dynamics. This limits their ability to generalize under out-of-distribution perturbations. To address this issue, we propose ELAN4D, an embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training framework that enhances VLA policies with future robot keypoint tracks as predictive spatio-temporal supervision. Using only forward kinematics from proprioceptive states, we derive 3D displacement tracks of robot keypoints, such as joints and the end-effector, with negligible preprocess cost. These tracks provide metric and compact supervision without requiring external trackers or reconstruction. A plug-and-play auxiliary branch with a lightweight track decoder injects this 4D signal into the action expert while preserving the pretrained vision-language backbone through gradient isolation. The track decoder is discarded during inference, leaving the base policy interface unchanged. Extensive experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0 and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that ELAN4D consistently improves over strong VLA baselines, achieving the best overall performance and substantial gains under out-of-distribution perturbations, including camera, background, and layout shifts. These results highlight the effectiveness of embodiment-centric 4D supervision for building more robust and generalizable manipulation policies.

</details>


### [230] [CoMo3R-SLAM: Collaborative Monocular Dense SLAM with Learned 3D Reconstruction Priors for Outdoor Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30488)
*Zhihao Cao,Qi Shao,Shuhao Zhai,Feng Tian,Anh Nguyen,Hesheng Wang,Baoru Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoMo3R-SLAM is a pioneering collaborative monocular dense RGB SLAM system for multi-robot mapping without depth sensors, utilizing learned 3D reconstruction priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing systems are limited by the costs and complexities of depth sensors. Collaborative dense SLAM with monocular cameras faces challenges due to scale ambiguity and unreliable data association in outdoor environments.

Method: The proposed system uses a prior-guided front-end for tracking and dense fusion, alongside a coordinator for pointmap matching, gauge synchronization, and global adjustment, all optimized for monocular input.

Result: CoMo3R-SLAM outperforms in ATE on Tanks and Temples scenes and shows competitive results on Waymo, achieving performance on par with RGB-D methods at 8 FPS.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the viability of using monocular RGB for effective collaborative dense SLAM in large outdoor environments, offering a significant advance in the field.

Abstract: Collaborative dense SLAM is essential for multi-robot teams to achieve scalable and consistent 3D perception across large-scale outdoor environments. Existing systems typically depend on depth sensors, incurring significant payload, power, and calibration costs. Monocular RGB cameras are a lightweight alternative, but collaborative monocular dense SLAM remains difficult due to scale ambiguity, unreliable inter-agent data association, especially in outdoor scenes where low overlap and repetitive structures make traditional feature matching unreliable, motivating robust geometric information. We propose CoMo3R-SLAM, the first collaborative monocular dense RGB SLAM system that leverages robust learned feed-forward 3D reconstruction priors for outdoor multi-agent mapping. Each agent runs a prior-guided front-end for real-time tracking and local dense fusion, while a coordinator performs dense pointmap matching for cross-agent verification, closed-form Sim(3) gauge synchronization, and GPU-accelerated global bundle adjustment with segment-level depth optimization. Requiring neither depth sensors nor parametric intrinsics, our system produces robust cross-agent constraints and globally consistent metric maps from monocular RGB alone. On Tanks and Temples and Waymo sequences, CoMo3R-SLAM achieves the best ATE on three of four Tanks and Temples scenes and competitive Waymo accuracy, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art RGB-D methods while running online at 8 FPS.

</details>


### [231] [Physics-informed Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning under Hybrid Contact Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30503)
*Vittorio Giammarino,Anastasios Manganaris,Ahmed H. Qureshi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces contact-aware and hierarchical formulations to enhance the effectiveness of physics-informed goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (Pi-GCRL) in contact-rich manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to improve Pi-GCRL methods, which struggle with high-dimensional and contact-dependent dynamics that can degrade their performance in manipulation tasks.

Method: The paper proposes contact-aware and hierarchical approaches that selectively apply inductive biases inherent in physics-informed GCRL to better handle contact-rich scenarios.

Result: The proposed formulations show improved reliability in contact-rich tasks, addressing the degradation observed when applying existing Pi-GCRL methods naively.

Conclusion: The findings contribute to the advancement of Pi-GCRL in complex manipulation environments, marking progress in the field of goal-conditioned reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Learning to reach arbitrary goals from sparse feedback requires agents to infer a rich notion of reachability across state--goal pairs. Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) tackles this challenge by learning policies that generalize across goals, but this generalization becomes increasingly difficult as the underlying dynamics become high-dimensional, hybrid, or contact-dependent. To address this issue, physics-informed GCRL (Pi-GCRL) introduces optimal-control-inspired inductive biases into goal-conditioned value learning. While Pi-GCRL methods have proven effective in navigation and object-free goal-reaching domains, their reliability in contact-rich tasks remains unclear, where contact interactions induce hybrid dynamics, mode-dependent controllability, and nonsmooth value landscapes. In this work, we show that these structural properties can cause existing Pi-GCRL methods to degrade when applied naively to contact-rich manipulation. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce contact-aware and hierarchical formulations that apply physics-informed inductive biases selectively across the manipulation problem. Our results provide a principled step toward extending Pi-GCRL to contact-rich manipulation.

</details>


### [232] [VLM-GLoc: Vision-Language Model Enhanced Monte Carlo Localization for Robust Semantic Global Localization in Cluttered Quasi-Static Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30506)
*Shivendra Agrawal,Bradley Hayes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLM-GLoc introduces a hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization approach leveraging Vision-Language Models to improve global localization in complex indoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge of global localization in ambiguous, quasi-static indoor environments such as grocery stores and offices necessitates a more robust approach beyond traditional methods.

Method: VLM-GLoc combines hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization with open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models to enhance semantic observations and introduce an inverse semantic proposal mechanism.

Result: VLM-GLoc achieved 70% and 74% global localization success in a grocery store and lab space respectively, significantly exceeding traditional methods.

Conclusion: The use of Vision-Language Models in VLM-GLoc leads to improved localization performance in geometrically and semantically complex environments.

Abstract: Global localization in geometrically aliased, quasi-static environments such as grocery stores, offices, schools, and hospitals poses a significant challenge for mobile robots. Grocery stores with parallel aisles and a long tailed distribution of products, as well as offices and labs with repetitive furniture such as chairs, desks, monitors, and doors, exemplify common indoor environments that present geometric and even semantic ambiguity. Traditional approaches rely either on distinct geometric features or on domain-specific vision pipelines that struggle with long-tail semantic distributions and transient visual clutter. We present VLM-GLoc, a method for hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) that leverages open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a unified semantic observation front-end. We hypothesize a three-fold benefit from VLMs: (1) extracting highly discriminative rich text features, (2) implicit quality filtering of blurry or dynamic objects, and (3) permanence reasoning for targeted data augmentation. We introduce an inverse semantic proposal mechanism that seeds particles via text-to-map retrieval. Evaluated across two real-world environments with different characteristics and two different platforms: a 3,500 sq. ft. grocery store with a cellphone and a 3,700 sq. ft. lab space with a quadruped, VLM-GLoc achieves 70% and 74% global localization success respectively, substantially outperforming traditional geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

</details>


### [233] [ARISTO Hand: Sensing-Driven Distal Hyperextension for Fine-Grained Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30508)
*Aaron Kim,Dong Ho Kang,Mark Helwig,Mingyo Seo,Kazuto Yokoyama,Tetsuya Narita,Luis Sentis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The ARISTO Hand is a robotic hand designed for precise manipulation of thin objects with enhanced force perception through innovative sensing and actuation features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the manipulative capabilities of robotic hands for thin object handling, addressing the limitations of conventional mechanical and sensing systems.

Method: The ARISTO Hand employs tendon-driven actuation with active hyperextension and a hybrid sensing system featuring a rigid force-torque sensor and a soft tactile array.

Result: The hand achieved a 2.76x increase in pull-out force for objects between 1-20 mm in thickness while maintaining grasping ability, validated through force characterization and task performance.

Conclusion: The hybrid architecture enhances the performance of robotic hands in thin object manipulation and offers reliable contact force measurements, critical for effective grasping.

Abstract: Manipulating thin objects requires precise contact geometry and reliable force perception, yet many anthropomorphic robotic hands lack the mechanical and sensing capabilities needed for such interactions. We present the ARISTO Hand, a tendon-driven robotic hand that integrates active distal hyperextension with a hybrid fingertip-sensing architecture that combines a rigid, nail-mounted force-torque sensor and a soft capacitive tactile array. Active hyperextension enables controlled fingertip engagement beyond the kinematic limits of standard flexion, increasing pull-out force by 2.76x for object thicknesses of 1-20 mm while preserving the nominal grasp capability. The rigid nail-mounted sensor provides reliable force measurements during edge contacts, where the sensitivity of proprioceptive force estimation degrades as the contact geometry approaches kinematic singularities. We validate the proposed architecture through quantitative force characterization and a multi-stage SD card extraction and insertion task. Video and supplementary materials are available at: https://aristohand.github.io

</details>


### [234] [Any-ttach: Quick End-effector Swapping Enables Manipulation Dexterity with Simplicity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30569)
*Weizhe Ni,Jinzhou Li,Haoyu Li,Cody Andres Alessio-Bunnell,Wenjing Pan,Xianyi Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Any-ttach introduces a tool-centric approach to robotic manipulation, emphasizing end-effector swapping for enhanced dexterity and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The complexity of human hands is largely attributed to their role in tool use and creation, prompting a re-evaluation of robotic manipulation strategies.

Method: Any-ttach integrates an automatic tool-swapping mechanism, a device for collecting human demonstrations, and a task planning framework for tool-use skills.

Result: Experiments demonstrate improved reliability in tool swapping, enhanced efficiency in demonstrations, reduced variability in tool pose, and support for various tool-use skills.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that expanding robotic manipulation capabilities can be achieved through fast tool exchanges and adaptable end-effector modules, rather than solely relying on complex designs.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation dexterity is often pursued by building increasingly complex high-DoF multifingered hands. While many robotic hands are designed to replicate human morphology, the functional role of human hands suggests a different perspective: much of their complexity may exist to enable tool use and tool making. This observation motivates Any-ttach, a tool-centric manipulation framework that treats quick end-effector swapping as a mechanism for dexterity with simplicity. Any-ttach combines a low-cost automatic swapping mechanism for an open-close robot interface, a handheld device for collecting human demonstrations, and a task planning framework that composes learned, parameterized, and planned tool-use skills. The system supports diverse tools and end-effector modules, including daily tools, articulated tools such as scissors, Fin Ray fingers, and a low-cost anthropomorphic hand, through the same shared interface. Our experiments show that Any-ttach improves tool-swapping reliability, increases demonstration efficiency, reduces tool-pose variability, and supports diverse tool-use skills. In two long-horizon tasks, making a sandwich and preparing a cucumber, Any-ttach executes six tool-use subskills through end-effector switching and execution monitoring. These results suggest that robots can expand manipulation capability not only through more complex end-effectors, but also through rapidly exchangeable tools and end-effector modules. More details and videos are available at https://any-ttach.github.io/.

</details>


### [235] [Caspar: CUDA Accelerator for Symbolic Programming with Adaptive Reordering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30583)
*Emil Martens,Aaron Miller,Matias Varnum,Annette Stahl*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Caspar is a new library that enhances GPU accessibility for robotics, offering a nonlinear GPU solver that utilizes symbolic programming in Python to generate optimized CUDA kernels. It significantly outperforms existing bundle adjusters in speed, memory efficiency, and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind Caspar is to bridge the gap between high-level symbolic programming and efficient GPU computation, thereby making powerful GPU capabilities more accessible for various optimization problems in robotics.

Method: Caspar automatically generates optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions defined by users, utilizing the SymForce library for symbolic differentiation and seamless interaction with GPU runtimes. Users only need to define symbolic residual functions for nonlinear optimization tasks.

Result: Caspar demonstrates a performance improvement of 5 to 20 times compared to state-of-the-art bundle adjusters while consuming less memory and maintaining similar accuracy during tests on the Bundle Adjustment in the Large (BAL) dataset.

Conclusion: The implementation of Caspar showcases the advantages of symbolic GPU programming, highlighting its potential impact on computational efficiency in robotics-related optimization tasks.

Abstract: We present Caspar, a library that makes the power of modern GPUs more accessible in robotics and provides a state-of-the-art nonlinear GPU solver that can be applied to a wide range of different optimization problems. Caspar bridges the gap between expressive symbolic programming in Python and high-performance GPU runtimes in C++ by automatically generating optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions. Building on the SymForce library, users can easily define and combine symbolic expressions, including Lie group operations, to generate custom CUDA kernels. To use Caspar as a solver, users need only define the symbolic residual functions; Caspar then uses symbolic differentiation to generate the necessary GPU kernels and interfaces to perform nonlinear optimization.
  In this paper, we present the core components of Caspar and showcase its performance by performing bundle adjustment on the Bundle Adjustment in the Large (BAL) dataset. We benchmark Caspar against other state-of-the-art bundle adjusters and show that it is 5 to 20 times faster than the best alternative, requires less memory, and achieves similar accuracy. This illustrates the benefit of our symbolic GPU programming approach. Caspar is released as part of SymForce and is freely available at https://github.com/symforce-org/symforce

</details>


### [236] [ZAPS-DA: Zero-Phase Action Policy Smoothing with Decoupled Actor for Continuous Control in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30612)
*Faiq Shamass*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ZAPS-DA reduces action jitter in off-policy reinforcement learning frameworks without introducing phase lag or requiring post-processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency action jitter in off-policy reinforcement learning hinders practical deployment on physical actuators.

Method: ZAPS-DA combines an unmodified main actor with a decoupled actor trained on supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets from a replay buffer, using magnitude-matched MSE loss for zero-hyperparameter portability.

Result: ZAPS-DA achieved a 14-21x reduction in steering jitter and 3-5x in throttle jitter on MetaDrive without affecting task completion significantly; similar improvements were observed in the Webots environment with a notable reduction in task-failure rates.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively mitigates action jitter while maintaining performance, indicating its potential for practical applications.

Abstract: Continuous control policies trained with off-policy reinforcement learning frequently exhibit high-frequency action jitter, rendering direct deployment on physical actuators impractical. Post-hoc filtering attenuates jitter but introduces phase lag; embedding smoothness penalties in the actor's loss couples them with the RL gradient and conflates reward regression with over-aggressive smoothing. We present ZAPS-DA, a framework that reduces action jitter at deployment with negligible phase lag and no post-processing. ZAPS-DA pairs an unmodified main actor (trained by the base RL loss) with a separate decoupled actor trained via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets stored in the replay buffer. The deployed policy is the decoupled actor: a feed-forward map from the current observation to a smooth action, with no inference-time filter and no action-history input -- a mechanism we term causal distillation of a non-causal filter. A magnitude-matched MSE loss provides zero-hyperparameter portability across optimizer classes. Validated with Soft Actor-Critic and a Savitzky--Golay filter in two driving simulators using paired n=150 evaluation protocols: on MetaDrive, ZAPS-DA reduces steering jitter by 14--21x and throttle jitter by 3--5x (all $p < 10^{-4}$, Bonferroni-corrected) while matching task-completion (p=0.28 success, p=0.31 crash) at a 6.3% reward cost; on a custom Webots adaptive cruise control environment, the same SG configuration produces a Pareto improvement -- reward parity (p=0.121), 8--45x steering jitter reduction, and total task-failure rate reduced from 2.0% to 0.7%.

</details>


### [237] [Exploiting Chordal Sparsity for Globally Optimal Estimation with Factor Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30617)
*Avinash Subramanian,Connor Holmes,Timothy D. Barfoot,Frank Dellaert,Frederike Dümbgen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new procedure within the GTSAM framework to automatically create convex SDP relaxations for factor graphs, addressing challenges in robust state estimation in robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State estimation is essential for robotics, but local solvers can converge to poor minima, and convex relaxations are complex and costly.

Method: The paper develops a procedure for automatic construction of convex SDP relaxations for factor graphs and utilizes GTSAM's Bayes tree to decompose SDP problems for efficiency.

Result: The proposed method shows improved performance and speed compared to standard local solvers in two case studies involving 3D pose-graph SLAM and 2D localization problems.

Conclusion: The new SDP procedure significantly enhances state estimation efficiency while ensuring global solutions, making it a promising tool for robotics.

Abstract: Robust and efficient state estimation is crucial for perception, navigation, and control in robotics. State estimation problems are conveniently modeled using the factor-graph framework as enabled by modern software packages such as GTSAM or g2o. However, the standard solvers included in such frameworks are local and may converge to poor local minima, posing significant safety concerns. Conversely, techniques based on convex relaxations have been shown to provide a means of globally solving or certifying many state estimation problems. However, these relaxations 1) often require substantial effort to formulate, and 2) may incur significantly higher cost compared to efficient local solvers, as they require solving a large semidefinite program (SDP). In this work, we address both shortcomings by 1) creating a new procedure within the GTSAM framework for automatically constructing convex SDP relaxations for any factor graphs with common factor and variable types, and by 2) exploiting the Bayes tree constructions native to GTSAM to decompose the SDP problem, leading to significant speedup in solver time for chordally sparse problems. We demonstrate the favorable scaling of this structure-exploiting global estimator compared to standard local solvers for two case studies: A 3D pose-graph SLAM problem with a ring factor graph and a 2D localization problem with a chain factor graph. The software framework is available at https://github.com/borglab/gtsam.

</details>


### [238] [Bidirectional Incremental Generalized Hybrid A*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30647)
*Sidharth Talia,Oren Salzman,Siddhartha Srinivasa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents Bi-IGHA*, an extension of the Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* algorithm that improves kinodynamic planning in unstructured environments by coupling bidirectional search with hierarchical resolution, thereby enhancing efficiency and maintaining solution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of kinodynamic planning in complex and unstructured environments, particularly where traditional methods fail due to the curse of dimensionality and fixed discretization resolutions.

Method: Bi-IGHA* combines the Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* algorithm with a bidirectional search approach, allowing for searches over a hierarchy of resolutions while mitigating issues from frozen vertices that can obscure potential solutions.

Result: Bi-IGHA* maintains IGHA*'s guarantees regarding monotonic cost improvement and termination, demonstrating significantly reduced expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems compared to previous methods.

Conclusion: The empirical results confirm that Bi-IGHA* allows for effective kinodynamic planning in high-speed off-road autonomy with fewer expansions, achieving similar closed-loop performance as existing techniques.

Abstract: We focus on the problem of efficient anytime kinodynamic planning for systems with complex dynamics in unstructured environments that make precomputing motion primitives infeasible. Directly applying A* to such problems is computationally infeasible due to the curse of dimensionality. Methods such as Hybrid A* addressed this burden by discretizing the state space, but in turn creating a coupling between tree discovery and the discretization resolution. The Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* (IGHA*) performs search over a hierarchy of resolutions in an anytime fashion to break this coupling, by freezing vertices to use in later search iterations rather than pruning them. However, the frozen vertices can hide solution-supporting vertices from the search at a particular iteration. While classical bidirectional search is motivated by the reduction of search depth, extending IGHA* into the bidirectional setting (termed Bi-IGHA*) obtains additional benefit by fundamentally mitigating the behaviour induced by frozen vertices hiding solutions. We show that Bi-IGHA* preserves IGHA*'s guarantees on monotonic cost improvement and termination. We empirically show that Bi-IGHA* substantially reduces expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems, and achieves equivalent closed-loop performance with kinodynamic planning for high-speed off-road autonomy while requiring significantly fewer expansions. Website: https://personalrobotics.github.io/IGHAStar/biighastar.html

</details>


### [239] [Primitive Subspaces Mediate Few-Shot Transfer in VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30695)
*Anya Singh,Cabrel Happi,Jai Relan,Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method for teaching vision-language-action (VLA) policies new tasks using primitive-aware training to enhance transferability and efficiency in industrial settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the current limitations of VLA policies that require extensive fine-tuning for new tasks, reducing the cost and time in industrial applications.

Method: Two VLA architectures, OpenVLA and $π_{0.5}$, were trained on the REASSEMBLE dataset, comparing flat trajectory training to primitive-segmented episodes with language prompts, assessing few-shot transfer with varied demonstration counts.

Result: Primitive-trained models achieved 78% of fine-tuned performance with only 3 demonstrations, demonstrating a 3x efficiency improvement over flat-trained counterparts needing 10 demonstrations, validated across multiple experiments and datasets.

Conclusion: Primitive representations are essential for effective few-shot transfer, as evidenced by significant performance degradation when they are ablated, alongside addressing a critical methodological flaw in prior evaluations of chunked policies.

Abstract: Deploying vision-language-action (VLA) policies in industrial environments requires the ability to teach new tasks at low cost, a property current VLAs lack, since each new task requires fine-tuning. We investigate whether primitive-aware training produces a transferable artifact: a learned library of sub-skills that can be composed at inference time, conditioned on a small number of demonstrations, to perform tasks the policy was never trained on. We train two VLA architectures with different inductive biases, OpenVLA and $π_{0.5}$, on the REASSEMBLE contact-rich assembly dataset under matched LoRA fine-tuning recipes and locked hyperparameters, varying training between flat trajectories and primitive-segmented episodes with primitive-specific language prompts. We hold out 6 object-task combinations from training and evaluate few-shot transfer: models receive $m \in \{0, 1, 3, 5, 10\}$ demonstrations of a held-out task and attempt execution without weight updates. We replicate across three training seeds and validate on a second dataset (LIBERO-Long). Primitive-trained models reach 78% of fine-tuned upper-bound performance with only m=3 demonstrations, while flat-trained models require m=10 demonstrations to reach the same level -- a $3\times$ sample efficiency gap that replicates across seeds, architectures, and datasets. To establish causation, we ablate the primitive-decodable subspace of hidden states and show few-shot transfer degrades by 32 percentage points while ablating a random subspace of equal dimensionality has no effect, indicating primitive representations are causally necessary rather than incidentally correlated with transfer. We identify and correct a methodological pitfall in evaluating chunked policies: family-wise inflation of single-step action-range gates produces order-of-magnitude higher false-failure rates against ground-truth human demonstrations.

</details>


### [240] [Geometry-Aware Control Barrier Functions for Collision Avoidance via Bernstein Polynomial Approximations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30696)
*Siwon Jo,Yanze Zhang,Yupeng Yang,Wenhao Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel geometry-aware Control Barrier Function using Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields for improved robot navigation in complex environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CBF methods face limitations with common shape surrogates leading to conservative or inefficient safety constraints in unstructured environments.

Method: The proposed CBF utilizes Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields for a unified representation of robots and obstacles, enabling precise control enforcement.

Result: The method shows improved efficiency and safety guarantees in both single and multi-robot navigation scenarios through simulation.

Conclusion: The new geometry-aware CBF enhances navigation safety and performance in various environments, surpassing previous conservative approaches.

Abstract: Safe navigation often relies on well-defined conditions based on the shape of robots and obstacles, and can be challenging when they have irregular geometries. While Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) offer an efficient mechanism to enforce safe set forward invariance, common shape surrogates (e.g., spheres or super-ellipsoids) either are overly conservative in unstructured scenes or require many local primitives, which inflates constraint counts and degrades real-time performance. In this paper, we introduce a novel geometry-aware Control Barrier Function (CBF) based on Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs). It provides a unified way to represent the obstacles and robots, so as to represent the barrier function with a unified minimum distance. Benefiting from the differentiability of the Bernstein polynomials, one can easily enforce the control constraints in a closed loop. We validate the method's efficiency and performance to guarantee safety in single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance via simulations under different environments.

</details>


### [241] [GSAM: A Generalizable and Safe Robotic Framework for Articulated Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30740)
*Beichen Shao,Mengying Xie,Heng Su,Wanyi Zhang,Mingyan Li,Yan Ding,Fausto Giunchiglia,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GSAM is a novel framework aimed at enhancing articulated object manipulation in service robots by improving generalization and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for articulated object manipulation often fail to generalize well across diverse object types and can result in destructive interactions due to overlooked complexities.

Method: GSAM utilizes a vision-based perceiver for kinematic parameter generation, a fine-tuned VLM for commonsense reasoning refinement, and designs a constraint generator for safe interactions, followed by a kinematic-aware manipulation planner.

Result: Compared to the best baseline, GSAM decreases standard deviation by 3.1% and elevates the manipulation success rate by 36.0% across 50 hinge tasks and diverse configurations.

Conclusion: GSAM demonstrates improved generalization and interaction safety for robotic manipulation of articulated objects in practical scenarios.

Abstract: Articulated object manipulation is a unique challenge for service robots. Existing methods employ end-to-end policy learning, visionmotion planning, and large-language/visual-language model (LLM/VLM), but often overlook the diversity of articulated objects and the complexity of interactions between end-effector and handle, leading to limited generalization and destructive collisions. To address this, we propose GSAM, a generalizable and safe robotic framework for articulated object manipulation. Specifically, a vision-based perceiver generates the kinematic parameters.
