These inverse-dynamics-derived signals are used to optimize the planned trajectory, turning future forecasting from passive scene anticipation into actionable planning guidance. A lightweight closed-loop refinement module further improves long-horizon consistency by reusing the optimized trajectory for another round of future-aware reasoning. By introducing inverse dynamics into latent future reasoning, IDOL tightens the coupling between world modeling and planning. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 benchmarks show that IDOL achieves state-of-the-art performance among comparable methods.

</details>


### [272] [Batched Differentiable Rigid Body Dynamics in PyTorch for GPU-Accelerated Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31481)
*Yue Wang,Yanran Xu,Wenbo Wu,Chuanhang Qiu,Zhaoxing Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BARD, a PyTorch implementation of rigid-body dynamics, optimizes GPU performance for robot control, significantly outperforming CPU-based libraries like Pinocchio in throughput while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottlenecks in GPU-based training pipelines caused by CPU-bound libraries in large-scale reinforcement learning applications.

Method: Developed BARD using Featherstone's algorithms with optimizations including lazy-evaluation caching, matmul-free joint transforms, and level-parallel propagation for efficient GPU computation.

Result: BARD achieves up to 64x higher throughput for Forward Kinematics and 63x for Jacobians over Pinocchio on an NVIDIA H200, validated through high-accuracy gradient identification on robotic models.

Conclusion: BARD integrates successfully into existing training pipelines, providing substantial efficiency gains while maintaining numerical accuracy, and is available for public use.

Abstract: As robot control shifts toward large-scale reinforcement learning with in-loop dynamics computation, the community's reliance on CPU-bound libraries such as Pinocchio creates a throughput bottleneck in GPU-based training pipelines. We present BARD (Batched Articulated Rigid-body Dynamics), a self-contained PyTorch implementation of Featherstone's rigid-body dynamics algorithms, optimized for batched GPU evaluation and automatic differentiation. Three design choices make this efficient: a tiered lazy-evaluation cache that avoids redundant tree traversals, matmul-free joint transforms via pre-computed Rodrigues constants, and level-parallel propagation that reduces sequential operations to tree-depth batched steps. On five robot models (7-23 DOFs), BARD matches Pinocchio numerically while reaching up to 64x higher throughput for Forward Kinematics and 63x for Jacobians at batch size 4096 on an NVIDIA H200. We validate differentiability through gradient-based system identification on a 7-DOF manipulator, recovering link masses to 1.24% mean error under 5% torque noise, and integrate BARD into an Isaac Lab AMP training pipeline for an 11-DOF spined quadruped with 4096 parallel environments, where it is 8.5x faster than Pinocchio and 2.0x faster than ADAM for in-loop dynamics. BARD is open-sourced at: https://github.com/YueWang996/bard-pytorch-dynamics.

</details>


### [273] [Learning Controlled Separation of Small Objects Between Two Fingers with a Tactile Skin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31486)
*Ulf Kasolowsky,Berthold Bäuml*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper addresses the controlled separation of small objects using a robotic hand, leveraging tactile feedback instead of vision, and demonstrates improved performance with higher resolution tactile sensors compared to traditional joint sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the robotic hand's ability to effectively separate small objects through tactile sensing without the reliance on visual input, providing real-world application potential.

Method: The task is approached through reinforcement learning in simulation with a spatially-resolved tactile skin, training a separation policy that utilizes tactile feedback for accurate object handling.

Result: Experimental results indicate that higher resolution tactile sensors significantly improve separation performance, with a 20% enhancement observed with a 4x4 sensor compared to only using joint sensors.

Conclusion: The research confirms the feasibility of effective controlled separation of small objects using tactile information and demonstrates a successful transfer of the learned policy from simulation to the physical robotic system.

Abstract: We introduce and solve the novel task of controlled separation of small objects with two fingers of a multi-purpose robotic hand: after grasping into a box of small objects, the task is to drop as many of them until a desired number remains between the fingers. The objects are small compared to the width of the fingers but also in absolute terms. In our case little pellets with a diameter of only 6mm are handled. We show that the task can be performed purely tactile (no vision) using a spatially-resolved tactile skin on a fingertip. The separation policy is trained in simulation via reinforcement learning using a straightforward sparse reward, which basically checks if the desired number of objects is reached. In simulation experiments, we provide an exhaustive analysis of the benefits of using spatially-resolved tactile feedback: while an ideal (high-resolution) tactile sensor allows solving the task almost perfectly, a sensor with lower spatial resolution (here 4x4 taxels) still leads to an improvement of up to 20% compared to using only the fingers' joint sensors. For this analysis, we further train an estimator alongside the policy that predicts the ground truth contact positions. Finally, we demonstrate the successful sim-to-real transfer for the DLR-Hand II equipped with a tactile skin.

</details>


<div id='cs.AR'></div>

# cs.AR [[Back]](#toc)

### [274] [FREESS: A Web-Based Educational Simulator for a RISC-V-Inspired Superscalar Processor with Tomasulo-Style Dynamic Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30377)
*Roberto Giorgi,Miquel Moretó Planas*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: FREESS is an open-source simulator for RISC-V-inspired superscalar processors, enhancing understanding of instruction-level parallelism through detailed state visualization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide an educational tool that aids in understanding superscalar processor architectures and instruction-level parallelism.

Method: FREESS offers a cycle-by-cycle simulation of processor operations, exposing various hardware state components and allowing easy modification of runtime parameters.

Result: The simulator allows comprehensive visualization of processor states and supports comparative analysis of different superscalar architectures.

Conclusion: FREESS has been effective in teaching Advanced Computer Architecture for 15 years and remains accessible as an open-source project.

Abstract: FREESS (Free Educational Superscalar Simulator) is an open-source teaching environment for instruction-level parallelism in a RISC-V-inspired superscalar processor. It provides a compact, cycle-by-cycle view of register renaming, issue, execution, write-back, commit, and memory ordering in a Tomasulo-style machine. The simulator exposes the register map, free pool, instruction window, reorder buffer, and load/store queues in one textual representation, so the evolution of the hardware state can be followed on screen and reproduced on paper. Runtime parameters such as issue width, queue sizes, and functional-unit latencies can be changed easily, enabling direct comparison among alternative superscalar organizations. The tool has supported Advanced Computer Architecture teaching for about fifteen years and is publicly available on GitHub.

</details>


### [275] [Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30571)
*Josef Chen*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: This study analyzes the memory-bandwidth limitations of physical AI systems during single-stream, batch-1 autoregressive decoding across different NVIDIA GPUs, revealing that faster memory does not yield proportional latency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the performance limitations of physical AI systems, focusing on their inference workloads compared to cloud LLMs, and analyze the impact of memory bandwidth on decoding latency.

Method: Measure batch-1 decoding performance of three GQA transformers on four NVIDIA GPUs, evaluating context lengths and peak HBM bandwidth, alongside a CUDA Graphs A/B experiment to analyze launch-side overhead effects.

Result: Memory utilization varies across GPUs; high peak bandwidth does not equate to linear latency gains. H100 GPU shows 27% efficiency at its analytic memory floor while L4 GPU achieves 81% at a lower context length. CUDA Graphs improves latency on H100 by 1.259x but only 1.028x on L4, indicating hidden overhead on faster GPUs.

Conclusion: Memory optimizations are more beneficial when runtime effectively utilizes them; optimizing for weight-traffic reduction does not guarantee expected improvements, as seen with various quantization approaches.

Abstract: Physical AI systems, including robots, autonomous vehicles, embodied agents and edge copilots, often run a different inference workload from cloud LLM serving: single-stream, batch-1 autoregressive decode, where one robot, camera feed or user session waits on the next token. This workload is usually described as memory-bandwidth-bound. Each decode step streams model weights and the active KV cache, so latency should scale with peak HBM bandwidth. We show that this account is true but incomplete. We measure batch-1 decode for three 7 to 8B-class GQA transformers across four NVIDIA GPUs: H100 SXM5, A100-80GB SXM4, L40S and L4. We evaluate context lengths from 2048 to 16384, producing 44 valid cells under a controlled bf16 SDPA setup. The achieved fraction of peak HBM bandwidth falls as peak bandwidth rises. On the headline Qwen-2.5-7B ctx=2048 cell, an L4 reaches roughly 81 percent of its analytic memory floor, while an H100 reaches only 27 percent. Physical-AI decode is memory-dominated, but faster memory does not translate into proportional latency gains. We test the missing term with a CUDA Graphs A/B experiment. On H100 at ctx=2048, CUDA Graphs improves decode latency by 1.259x across N=10 fresh sessions, with a 95 percent bootstrap confidence interval of 1.253 to 1.267. On L4, the same intervention gives only 1.028x. This isolates a launch-side overhead that becomes visible on fast GPUs but remains mostly hidden on slower, bandwidth-bound GPUs. The deployment implication is that memory savings matter only when the runtime realises them. On L4, bf16 decode sits close to the memory floor, but common quantised paths do not recover the expected 4x weight-traffic reduction: bnb-nf4 reaches 59.36 ms/step and AutoAWQ+Marlin reaches 45.24 ms/step from a 62.32 ms bf16 baseline. GPTQ+ExLlamaV2, with Ada-tuned int4 kernels, reaches 17.36 ms/step.

</details>


### [276] [A Reconfigurable Computing In-Memory Macro with Charge-sharing-based Weighted Accumulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30814)
*Junyi Yang,Shuai Dong,Zhengnan Fu,Hongyang Shang,Arindam Basu*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: This paper presents a multi-bit reconfigurable SRAM-based computing array that addresses key challenges such as ADC overhead, latency, and limited voltage in analog computing-in-memory systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve efficiency in SRAM-based analog computing-in-memory by overcoming significant ADC overhead, high latency for multi-bit inputs, and limited bitline voltage.

Method: The proposed architecture is a 256x128 in-memory computing array, integrating innovations like a low-overhead IMADC, a latency-reducing BSCHA, and a dual-8T bitcell for improved weight storage and bitline voltage.

Result: The IMADC achieves only 3% area overhead with a 9x improvement over previous designs; the BSCHA reduces latency by 1.9x compared to PWM and 6.6x compared to bit-slicing; the dual-8T bitcell enhances current linearity by 7x and increases usable read voltage by 3.5x.

Conclusion: The proposed solutions significantly enhance the performance of SRAM-based analog computing-in-memory, addressing critical challenges and enabling more efficient computation.

Abstract: SRAM-based analog computing-in-memory demonstrates outstanding efficiency. However, it faces three critical challenges: significant ADC overhead, high latency for multi-bit inputs, and limited read bitline voltage. To address these issues, this work proposes a multi-bit highly reconfigurable 256x128 in-memory computing array supporting 1-7b input, 2-4b weight, and 1-7b output. Three key innovations are introduced: 1) The IMADC occupies only 3% area overhead, achieving a 9x improvement compared to previous IMADC; 2) The BSCHA reduces latency by 1.9x and 6.6x compared to traditional pulse-width modulation (PWM) and bit-slicing modes, respectively; 3) A dual-8T bitcell enabling ternary weight storage through a decoupled read path, integrated with a read wordline under-driven cascode technique, improves linearity of unit discharge current by 7x and increases the usable read bitline voltage by 3.5x.

</details>


### [277] [HE^2: A Communication-Light Heterogeneous Architecture for Efficient Fully Homomorphic Encryption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31004)
*Shangyi Shi,Husheng Han,Zhaoxuan Kan,Yinghao Yang,Jianan Mu,Tenghui Hua,Ge Yu,Xinyao Zheng,Ling Liang,Zidong Du,Xing Hu*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: This paper introduces $HE^2$, a FHE accelerator that reduces communication overhead in CKKS by optimizing dataflow and architecture for enhanced performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high computational and memory costs in CKKS while reducing inefficiencies in existing accelerator designs.

Method: $HE^2$ employs dataflow graph optimization and architecture co-design to minimize communication between operators and leverages group-level pipelined execution.

Result: $HE^2$ achieves a 1.66x speedup and 9.23x lower EDAP compared to current accelerators, with communication stalls only affecting 6.67% of latency.

Conclusion: The proposed design effectively mitigates communication bottlenecks and enhances overall performance in CKKS applications.

Abstract: CKKS, an emerging fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) scheme, has been promising in privacy-preserving applications by enabling SIMD fixed-point computations on ciphertexts. Despite its strong security guarantees, CKKS involves both compute-intensive operators (ComOps) with high computational cost and memory-intensive operators (MemOps) with large memory footprints, making existing ASIC-based or NMP-based acceleration approaches suffer from high hardware overhead and limited efficiency. This observation motivates the integration of the architectural advantages of both paradigms into a heterogeneous xPU (ASIC)-xMU (NMP) architecture. However, in such a design, frequent and long-latency heterogeneous communication caused by the dominant keyswitch operator remains a key performance bottleneck.
  In this paper, we propose $HE^2$, a communication-light xPU-xMU heterogeneous FHE accelerator with dataflow graph (DFG) optimization and architecture co-design. First, we observe that the majority of communication arises at the interface between ModUp/ModDown and neighboring MemOps. To address this, we propose a DFG-level optimization framework to fully exploit the ModUp/ModDown reduction potential of the hoisting algorithm by identifying parallel keyswitch blocks and fusing them for reduced communication frequency. Second, we design an efficient heterogeneous architecture that adopts a group-level pipelined execution to effectively hide communication latency by leveraging the inherent parallelism across decomposed groups. End-to-end evaluation results show that $HE^2$ achieves 1.66$\times$ speedup and 9.23$\times$ lower EDAP (Energy-Delay-Area Product) compared to the state-of-the-art accelerator, with communication stalls accounting for only 6.67% of the total latency.

</details>


### [278] [MixFP4: Enhancing NVFP4 with Adaptive FP4/INT4 Block Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31035)
*Jiaxiang Zou,Yonghao Chen,Ruilong Wu,Xinyu Chen*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: MixFP4 optimizes low-precision formats in large language models by utilizing two FP4 micro-formats per block, enhancing quantization robustness and accuracy with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the performance of low-precision formats in large language models that face mismatches in tensor statistics with single FP4 formats.

Method: MixFP4 utilizes a mixed micro-format that selects between two FP4 micro-formats (E2M1 and E1M2) per block without altering the existing execution path, using the existing scale hierarchy and repurposing the sign bit for metadata.

Result: MixFP4 demonstrates improved quantization robustness and accuracy compared to NVFP4 baselines, with only slight increases in area (3.1%) and power (1.5%) cost.

Conclusion: MixFP4 successfully enhances low-precision capabilities for large language models while maintaining efficiency in processing.

Abstract: As large language models continue to scale, fine-grained block-scaled low-precision formats such as NVFP4 are increasingly adopted for their substantial throughput and memory benefits. However, a single FP4 micro-format often mismatches heterogeneous block-level tensor statistics. To address this without changing the standard block-scaled MMA/GEMM execution path, we propose MixFP4, a mixed micro-format extension to NVFP4 that selects between two stored FP4 micro-formats (E2M1 and E1M2) per block. MixFP4 reuses NVFP4's scale hierarchy and encodes the format choice with zero additional metadata by repurposing the sign bit of the FP8 E4M3 block scale. By decoding both micro-formats into a unified internal E2M2 compute representation, MixFP4 avoids datapath duplication. Across representative LLM families, MixFP4 improves FP4 quantization robustness and accuracy over NVFP4 baselines with modest tensor-core overhead (3.1\% area, 1.5\% power).

</details>


### [279] [TRINE: A Token-Aware, Runtime-Adaptive FPGA Inference Engine for Multimodal AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.22867)
*Hyunwoo Oh,Hanning Chen,Sanggeon Yun,Yang Ni,Suyeon Jang,Behnam Khaleghi,Fei Wen,Mohsen Imani*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: TRINE is an FPGA-based accelerator that optimizes multimodal inference using a unified architecture and techniques like token pruning and dependency-aware layer offloading to significantly enhance performance while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal frameworks face challenges in performance and efficiency on embedded platforms due to diverse compute and memory patterns, necessitating a robust solution for real-time applications.

Method: TRINE employs a single-bitstream FPGA architecture that combines various layer types and supports runtime configuration of processing modes, implementing optimizations such as a two-stage top-k unit for token pruning and dependency-aware layer offloading.

Result: TRINE demonstrates significant performance improvements, achieving up to 22.57x latency reduction compared to RTX 4090 and 6.86x compared to Jetson Orin Nano, with substantial efficiency gains from its optimization techniques.

Conclusion: With minimal accuracy loss (<2.5%) post-quantization, TRINE delivers competitive latency and energy efficiency for unified tasks across vision, language, and graph domains within a single bitstream.

Abstract: Multimodal stacks that mix ViTs, CNNs, GNNs, and transformer NLP strain embedded platforms because their compute/memory patterns diverge and hard real-time targets leave little slack. TRINE is a single-bitstream FPGA accelerator and compiler that executes end-to-end multimodal inference without reconfiguration. Layers are unified as DDMM/SDDMM/SpMM and mapped to a mode-switchable engine that toggles at runtime among weight/output-stationary systolic, 1xCS SIMD, and a routable adder tree (RADT) on a shared PE array. A width-matched, two-stage top-k unit enables in-stream token pruning, while dependency-aware layer offloading (DALO) overlaps independent kernels across reconfigurable processing units to sustain utilization. Evaluated on Alveo U50 and ZCU104, TRINE reduces latency by up to 22.57x vs. RTX 4090 and 6.86x vs. Jetson Orin Nano at 20-21 W; token pruning alone yields up to 7.8x on ViT-heavy pipelines, and DALO contributes up to 79% throughput improvement. With int8 quantization, accuracy drops remain <2.5% across representative tasks, delivering state-of-the-art latency and energy efficiency for unified vision, language, and graph workloads-in one bitstream.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [280] [Kernel Foundry: A Diagnosis-driven Evolutionary Kernel Optimizer with Multi-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30359)
*Zixuan Huang,Da Chen,Kecheng Huang,Lihao Yin,Xing Li,Huiling Zhen,Mingxuan Yuan,Zili Shao*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: Kernel Foundry is a novel framework for optimizing GPU kernels, enhancing both correctness and performance through evolutionary techniques and feedback mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges in generating high-performance GPU kernels that are both correct and optimized for hardware, as current large language models struggle with these requirements.

Method: Kernel Foundry utilizes a diagnosis-driven evolutionary framework that combines expert guidance, retrieval-augmented initialization, and multi-island evolutionary search to refine GPU kernels with diagnostic feedback.

Result: Experiments conducted with KernelBench demonstrate that Kernel Foundry significantly enhances both the correctness and performance of GPU kernels compared to strong baseline methods, achieving up to 100% correctness on Level~2.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the limitations of existing approaches, showing that evolutionary strategies combined with a centralized library of optimization knowledge can lead to significantly optimized GPU kernels.

Abstract: Generating high-performance GPU kernels remains challenging due to the need for both correctness and hardware-aware optimization. While large language models (LLMs) show promise in code generation, they often fail to produce kernels that are both correct and efficient.
  We propose Kernel Foundry, a diagnosis-driven evolutionary framework for automatic GPU kernel optimization. Our method combines expert-guided, retrieval-augmented initialization with a multi-island evolutionary search, where candidate kernels are iteratively refined using structured diagnostic feedback. A centralized experience library accumulates reusable optimization knowledge to guide subsequent evolution, while explicit mechanisms prevent cheating behaviors that bypass kernel-level computation.
  Experiments on KernelBench show that our method consistently improves both correctness and performance over strong baselines, achieving up to 100% correctness on Level~2.

</details>


### [281] [Gradient-Free Training of Spiking Neural Networks via Low-Rank Evolution Strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30361)
*Dhruv Patankar,Sachit Ramesha Gowda*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper presents EGGROLL, a method that enables efficient training of Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) using Evolution Strategies (ES) while reducing computational costs significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The training of SNNs is complicated due to non-differentiability of the spike threshold, and while surrogate-gradient methods exist, they are incompatible with on-chip learning necessary for neuromorphic hardware.

Method: EGGROLL applies low-rank factorization to ES perturbations, lowering memory requirements from $	extmath{O}(mn)$ to $	extmath{O}(r(m+n))$, which enhances efficiency during the training of SNNs.

Result: Using EGGROLL with a Leaky Integrate-and-Fire SNN on the N-MNIST dataset, the method achieved 79.21% test accuracy and reduced per-generation wall-clock time by 2.23 times compared to full-rank ES.

Conclusion: EGGROLL effectively facilitates gradient-free training of SNNs while ensuring compatibility with neuromorphic hardware and achieving a beneficial accuracy-speed tradeoff.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer compelling energy efficiency on neuromorphic hardware, yet their training remains challenging because the discrete spike threshold is non-differentiable. Surrogate-gradient methods sidestep this by approximating the derivative, but they impose backpropagation infrastructure that is incompatible with on-chip learning. Evolution Strategies (\es) are a natural gradient-free alternative, yet their computational cost scales with the number of parameters, making them impractical for large weight matrices.
  We present a method for training SNNs using EGGROLL, a low-rank factorisation of ES perturbations that reduces per-generation memory from $\mathcal{O}(mn)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r(m{+}n))$. Combining EGGROLL with a Leaky Integrate-and-Fire SNN on N-MNIST, we demonstrate that gradient-free training achieves 79.21% test accuracy while reducing per-generation wall-clock time by 2.23$\times$ relative to full-rank ES. Our results demonstrate EGGROLL is viable for SNN training, with a clear accuracy-speed tradeoff, compatible with training on neuromorphic hardware without surrogate gradients.

</details>


### [282] [XOResNet: Exclusive-OR Meta-Residuals Facilitate Deep Spiking Neural Networks Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30362)
*Jianfang Wu,Junsong Wang*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces XOResNet, a deep spiking neural network that improves learning and representation through innovative shortcut connections and residuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the efficiency of deep SNNs by addressing shortcomings related to spike redundancy and information loss encountered in existing residual structures.

Method: The study proposes an OR-ADD shortcut connection for merging output spikes and XOR meta-residuals to reduce redundant learning in residual structures, leading to the construction of the XOResNet architecture.

Result: XOResNet outperforms state-of-the-art deep SNNs on multiple datasets, demonstrating improved learning capabilities and addressing prior challenges.

Conclusion: The findings affirm the effectiveness of the new architectural components in bolstering performance in deep SNNs, paving the way for advancements in neuromorphic systems.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) hold promise for demonstrating superior learning and representation capabilities in deep models. Given the tremendous success of ResNet in deep learning, it would naturally follow to train deep SNNs with residual learning. However, existing residual structures for constructing deep SNNs still present challenges of spike redundancy or information loss, as well as redundant learning. In the present study, we first aim to address issues of relative spike redundancy in identity mapping and information loss in non-identity mapping. To this end, we propose an OR-ADD (OA) shortcut connection to merge output spikes/currents from two branches in the residual structure. Furthermore, to mitigate redundant learning in the backbone branch of the residual structure, we introduce the concept of XOR meta-residuals, i.e., selecting pre-learning residuals using the Exclusive-OR (XOR) operation for the backbone branch. Finally, by integrating the OA shortcut and XOR meta-residuals, we devise the XOR residual block and further construct XOResNet with varying depths based on this block. Extensive experiments on four datasets, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and miniImageNet, show that the proposed XOResNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art deep SNNs optimized via gradient descent. These results validate the effectiveness of our OA shortcut and XOR meta-residual components in overcoming fundamental limitations of residual learning in SNNs, providing new architectural insights for building high-performance neuromorphic systems.

</details>


### [283] [Updating the standard neuron model in artificial neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30370)
*Raul Mohedano,Thomas Batard,Erik Velasco-Salido,Ramsses De Los Santos Mendoza,Jorge H. Martínez,Stacey Levine,Marcelo Bertalmío*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper critiques the simplistic point neuron model in artificial neural networks (ANNs) and replaces it with a more advanced cortical cell model, demonstrating significant improvements in performance without increasing parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inadequacies of the point neuron model in ANNs, which fails to accurately reflect complex neural processes observed in neuroscience.

Method: By substituting the traditional point neuron model in ANNs with a recent model of cortical cells, and conducting both theoretical analyses and experimental evaluations.

Result: The new model leads to enhancements in expressivity, robustness, learning speed, and reduces memorization needs as well as the amount of training data required.

Conclusion: Implementing a more realistic neural unit element in ANNs can significantly improve their performance while maintaining the same number of parameters.

Abstract: From their inception in the 1950s, artificial neural networks (ANNs) started using the so-called point neuron model then prevalent in neuroscience, hoping that this analogy would allow for a better emulation of brain function. Over the years the neuroscience literature has shown that the point neuron model is too simplistic to properly represent many fundamental neural processes; however, the standard neuron model in ANNs still remains the same. Here we substitute it by a very recent model of cortical cells and demonstrate through theoretical analyses and experimental results how, simply by using a more realistic neural unit element without augmenting the number of parameters, the resulting ANNs offer a number of important advantages that include increases in expressivity, robustness and learning speed, and a reduction in memorization and the amount of training data needed.

</details>


### [284] [From Mean-Field Limits to Semiclassical Concentration: Global Convergence of the Canonical Evolutionary Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30371)
*Matías Neto,Nicolás Garay,Luis Martí,Nayat Sanchez-Pi*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper explores global convergence in stochastic continuous optimization using Canonical Evolutionary Strategy (CES) through a mathematical analysis of evolutionary algorithms derived from a Schrödinger-type replicator-mutator equation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to analyze and establish a rigorous framework for understanding global convergence in evolutionary algorithms, specifically addressing challenges that arise in standard consensus-driven methods.

Method: The paper formulates CES as a controlled mathematical model and establishes a hierarchy from individual-based dynamics to a mean-field limit, focusing on the principal eigenfunction of the corresponding operator to explain global convergence.

Result: CES demonstrates a significant advantage in global optimization, particularly in high-dimensional scenarios, achieving lower residual errors in tests where traditional methods succumb to premature convergence.

Conclusion: The findings support a new perspective on global convergence in Evolution Strategies, emphasizing spectral concentration over consensus, thus offering a solid theoretical foundation without reliance on numerical heuristics.

Abstract: We address the issue of global convergence in stochastic continuous optimization. For that purpose, we formulate the Canonical Evolutionary Strategy (CES) as a controlled mathematical framework to analyze global convergence in evolutionary algorithms via the semiclassical limit of a Schr{ö}dinger-type replicator-mutator equation. We provide a rigorous hierarchy from a discrete individual-based dynamics to a deterministic mean-field limit, demonstrating that global convergence is governed by the principal eigenfunction of the underlying operator. This property, defined as Geometric Selection, naturally prioritizes robust, flat optima over narrow local traps, offering a mathematical justification for the ''survival of the flattest'' phenomenon. Moreover, unlike consensus-driven methods that are prone to premature variance collapse when the global minimizer resides outside the initial support, the replicator-mutator dynamics of CES facilitate intrinsic mass transport. High-dimensional benchmarks (d = 30) confirm this advantage, showing that CES achieves lower residual errors in shifted initialization scenarios where standard consensus-driven and gradient-based methods fail to migrate effectively. By shifting the focus from point-wise consensus to spectral concentration, our framework provides a robust theoretical foundation for global convergence in Evolution Strategies (ES) without the need for additional numerical heuristics.

</details>


### [285] [GP-GOMEA with GPU-Based Fitness Evaluations: Design and Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30954)
*Jasper Post,Johannes Koch,Anton Bouter,Tanja Alderliesten,Peter A. N. Bosman*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: GP-GOMEA is enhanced with a GPU-accelerated fitness evaluation system, improving performance on symbolic regression tasks, particularly for larger datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational limitations of GP-GOMEA in symbolic regression, enabling its application to larger datasets and complex models.

Method: Introduces a GPU-based fitness evaluation scheme and a GPU-friendly representation of GP-GOMEA's individuals to leverage parallelism.

Result: Significant improvements in evaluation throughput, leading to better performance on standard symbolic regression benchmarks, especially with larger datasets and populations.

Conclusion: The enhancements facilitate systematic analysis of GP-GOMEA's challenges and enable it to effectively regress extensive Feynman equations.

Abstract: GP-GOMEA is a state-of-the-art evolutionary algorithm for symbolic regression, known for discovering small and interpretable models. However, its computational cost remains substantial, limiting its applicability to larger datasets and more complex target expressions. In contrast, the rise of modern subsymbolic approaches, particularly deep learning, has been driven largely by the massive parallelism offered by GPUs. In this work, we take the first major step toward a fully GPU-accelerated GP-GOMEA by introducing a GPU-based fitness evaluation scheme. We design a GPU-friendly representation of GP-GOMEA's template-based individuals and a corresponding evaluation strategy that exploits the inherent parallelism of population-based search. This substantially increases evaluation throughput, enabling orders of magnitude more evaluations within the same time budget. Across four standard symbolic regression benchmarks, this increased evaluation capacity yields performance improvements, particularly for larger datasets and larger population sizes. Moreover, the ability to efficiently evaluate much larger datasets and more complex templates enables analyses that were previously infeasible, allowing us to systematically analyze what makes expressions increasingly difficult for GP-GOMEA, providing new insights into how expression structure affects search difficulty. Finally, for the first time, this expanded capability allows a problem-agnostic evolutionary algorithm to reliably regress one of the largest Feynman equations within four hours.

</details>


### [286] [Linear Ordering Problem: Time for a Change](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31051)
*Fabrizio Fagiolo,Marco Baioletti,Valentino Santucci*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper addresses the Linear Ordering Problem (LOP) by introducing a new benchmark suite from current economic data and an algorithm that produces diverse high-quality solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current algorithms for the Linear Ordering Problem are based on outdated data and struggle with the existence of multiple global optima.

Method: Introduction of a benchmark suite based on recent economic data and an algorithm utilizing advanced LOP metaheuristics to generate diverse sets of solutions and quality metrics.

Result: Experiments were conducted showing results on the new benchmark under both traditional single-solution and multi-solution settings.

Conclusion: The study enhances LOP solution methods by providing updated benchmarks and enhances solution diversity, addressing both quality and practical applicability.

Abstract: The Linear Ordering Problem (LOP) is a fundamental combinatorial optimization problem with important applications in areas such as economics, social choice, and machine learning. Its most prominent use is the triangulation of economic input-output tables, which helps identify critical industries in an economy. Most existing algorithms have been evaluated on benchmarks derived from outdated macroeconomic data, which no longer reflect the structure of contemporary economies. Furthermore, LOP instances often exhibit many distinct global optima that can differ substantially from one another, creating challenges for applications that rely on a single solution. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel benchmark suite derived from up-to-date real-world economic data and an algorithmic scheme that leverages state-of-the-art LOP metaheuristics to generate diverse sets of high-quality solutions, together with metrics for assessing both quality and diversity. Experiments were conducted to report results on the proposed benchmark suite under both the traditional single-solution setting and the newly introduced multi-solution scenario

</details>


### [287] [Developing a novel Comorbidities Index for predicting 10-year mortality in Prostate Cancer patients: A computational data-driven approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31213)
*Davide Farinati,Francesco Barletta,Paolo Zaurito,Simone Scuderi,Nicholas Raison,Alejandro Granados,Prokar Dasgupta,Giorgio Gandaglia,Alberto Briganti*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The study develops a new comorbidity index for prostate cancer patients undergoing radical prostatectomy, enhancing predictions of ten-year survival and treatment decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the assessment of ten-year mortality risk in prostate cancer patients, addressing limitations of the original Charlson Comorbidities Index that may not reflect current prognoses.

Method: A data-driven framework employing Population-Based Bio-Inspired Algorithms (PBBIAs) to recalibrate comorbidity weights and optimize for ten-year survival discrimination through various optimization strategies including Genetic Programming.

Result: The algorithms GA, FST-PSO, and SLIM provided enhanced predictive performance over the original CCI and a revised version, especially when incorporating prostate cancer-specific factors, evidenced by a Concordance Index improvement of up to 0.1.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a more accurate and interpretable tool for patient selection for radical prostatectomy in prostate cancer treatment.

Abstract: The Charlson Comorbidities Index (CCI) is a weighted additive index widely used to estimate ten-year mortality risk, but its original weights may not reflect contemporary prognoses. This limitation is critical in Prostate Cancer (PCa), where radical treatment is recommended only for patients with a life expectancy of at least ten years. For candidates eligible for Radical Prostatectomy (RP), accurate estimation of ten-year other-cause mortality is essential to balance oncological benefit against competing risks and avoid overtreatment. We propose a data-driven framework to derive a comorbidity index tailored to PCa patients considered for RP. Using a retrospective single-institution cohort, we apply Population-Based Bio-Inspired Algorithms (PBBIAs) to recalibrate comorbidity weights and evolve alternative symbolic formulations optimized for ten-year survival discrimination. We compared six optimization strategies, including symbolic regression approaches based on Genetic Programming (GP), population-based metaheuristics, clinically validated baselines, and survival prediction models. Results show that GA, FST-PSO, and SLIM outperform both the original CCI and the PCCI, particularly when PCa-specific variables are included, improving the Concordance Index by up to 0.1. GPLearn yields compact and interpretable models with competitive performance. Overall, the proposed approach provides an updated and interpretable tool to improve patient selection for RP.

</details>


### [288] [Memristor-Based Spiking Neural Network Accelerator for Bio-inspired Interception Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31299)
*Qianhou Qu,Sheng Lu,Liuting Shang,Jaihan Utailawon,Sungyong Jung,Qilian Liang,Chenyun Pan*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper proposes an analog memristor-based spiking neural network (SNN) accelerator to enhance energy efficiency in neural computation, outperforming a digital SNN on energy consumption and delay in a predator-prey task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve energy efficiency in spiking neural networks by overcoming limitations of traditional GPU and CPU implementations.

Method: An analog memristor-based SNN accelerator is developed that integrates in-memory computation with analog integrate-and-fire neurons, alongside a digital SNN accelerator for comparison.

Result: The analog SNN accelerator exhibited a mean squared error of 0.004 in a tracking task and achieved 12.7 times lower energy consumption and 1.26 times lower delay compared to the digital version.

Conclusion: Memristor-based neuromorphic circuits show significant potential for energy-efficient processing in real-time applications.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) provide event-driven and low-power computation inspired by biological neural systems, but current implementations rely on von Neumann graphics processing units (GPUs) and central processing units (CPUs) platforms, where memory and computation bottlenecks limit energy efficiency. To address this challenge, this paper proposes an analog memristor-based spiking neural network (SNN) accelerator that integrates in-memory synaptic computation with analog integrate-and-fire (IF) neurons, eliminating multi-transistor CMOS synapse circuits and enabling asynchronous event-driven operation at the 45nm technology node. Additionally, a digital SNN accelerator is designed and optimized at the 5 nm technology node for comparison. The proposed architecture is evaluated using a predator-prey tracking task that emulates pursuit behavior. In this task, the analog SNN accelerator's inference closely matches the ideal software inference with a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.004. HSPICE simulation results show that the proposed analog SNN accelerator achieves 12.7 times lower energy consumption and 1.26 times lower delay compared to the digital baseline, demonstrating the potential of memristor-based neuromorphic circuits for energy-efficient real-time edge intelligence.

</details>


### [289] [Institutions and the transmission of upper-tail human capital: scientific lineages across a millennium](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31470)
*Hiroyuki Chuma,Kanji Otsuka,Yoichi Sato*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper examines the institutional transmission of upper-tail human capital over a millennium, revealing significant changes in mentorship networks and their implications for knowledge growth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how knowledge transmission institutions contributed to the cumulative nature of useful knowledge beyond mere discovery.

Method: Analysis of 470,000 mentor-student records from Wikidata, creating a directed acyclic graph of 25.5 million paths to trace historical knowledge transfers among scholars.

Result: Identification of two key institutional transitions: a concentration of scholarly lineage through Leibniz in the 17th century and upstream convergence on early Islamic and Byzantine scholars around the 12th-13th centuries, ending at the 'Monastery Wall.'

Conclusion: Transmission, rather than mere possession of knowledge, is crucial for generating spillovers that enable growth, with the study introducing a new method for analyzing large-scale academic mentorship networks.

Abstract: What made useful knowledge cumulative was not discovery alone but the institutions that transmitted it. We provide the first exhaustive structural measurement of the network through which upper-tail human capital passed from master to student across a millennium. Using 470,000 mentor-student records from Wikidata (which integrates the Mathematics Genealogy Project and MacTutor Archive), and all 64 historical Fields Medalists as a fixed, ex ante tracer set, backward traversal yields a directed acyclic graph of 25.5 million paths reaching 57 generations. We document two institutional transitions. First, a 17th-century watershed concentrates lineage traffic on Leibniz: 47 of 64 lineages pass through him with a 10:1 downstream-to-upstream ratio, and seven independent attributes -- learned-society membership (a 46-fold rise per scholar), field, language, employer, institutional diversification, student production, and diffusion entropy -- re-organize coherently across the same window. This is the network signature of Mokyr's Republic of Letters, and it reframes the Newton-Leibniz priority dispute as a distinction between the possession and the transmission of upper-tail human capital: it is transmission that generates the spillovers on which growth depends. Second, 84% of lineages converge upstream on five 12th-13th-century Islamic and Byzantine scholars before terminating at an 11th-century boundary -- the ``Monastery Wall'' -- at which personal academic mentorship first becomes record-generating in Europe. Our claims are descriptive-structural, not causal. Because exhaustive traversal at this scale defeats standard tools, we also contribute a deterministic, algebraic graph-traversal instrument whose measurement bias we characterize in closed form, and report one emergent property of independent methodological interest.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [290] [Gait2Hip-60: A Unified Deep Learning Benchmark for Predicting Hip Muscle Forces and Joint Moments from Multi-Cadence Gait Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30374)
*Jiaqi Zhang,Ji Hou,Qing Sun,Xianzhi Gao,Bo Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study develops a deep learning framework for predicting hip dynamics from gait kinematics, comparing three models, with the Transformer model yielding the best results in both healthy subjects and a cohort with osteonecrosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to create a more efficient method for estimating hip muscle forces and joint moments during gait, moving beyond traditional, time-intensive musculoskeletal simulations, particularly for clinical applications.

Method: A deep learning framework was created to predict hip muscle forces and joint moments from lower-limb gait kinematics using LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba models. Data from 60 healthy adults was used, focusing on ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles as inputs and OpenSim-derived outputs.

Result: The Transformer model outperformed the other models in predicting hip muscle forces and joint moments in healthy subjects, and showed moderate predictive capability in an external cohort of patients with osteonecrosis.

Conclusion: The study concludes that estimating hip dynamics from gait kinematics is feasible, identifies the Transformer model as the most effective, and underscores the necessity for additional validation and generalization efforts before clinical implementation.

Abstract: Estimating hip muscle forces and joint moments during gait typically relies on musculoskeletal simulation, which is informative but time-consuming and difficult to apply in clinical settings. This study developed a deep learning framework to predict these hip dynamics parameters directly from lower-limb gait kinematics and compared three representative sequence models under a unified protocol. Gait data were collected from 60 healthy adults under three metronome-guided cadence conditions. Ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles were used as inputs, and OpenSim-derived hip muscle forces and hip joint moments were used as reference outputs. Three deep learning models of LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba were trained and evaluated using the same subject-level split, preprocessing pipeline, and metrics. The best model was then directly tested on an external cohort of 9 patients with osteonecrosis of the femoral head (ONFH) without retraining. In the healthy-subject benchmark, Transformer achieved the best subject-level mean performance for both hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.33 N/kg, MAE = 0.57 N/kg, R2 = 0.819) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.11 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.07 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.862), with similar advantages across walking cadences. In zero-shot external validation, Transformer retained moderate predictive ability in ONFH for hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.51 N/kg, MAE = 0.70 N/kg, R2 = 0.537) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.17 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.12 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.569). These findings support the feasibility of estimating hip dynamics from gait kinematics, identify Transformer as a strong baseline, and highlight the need for broader pathological validation and improved generalization before clinical application.

</details>


### [291] [Unicorn: Scaling High-Dimensional Time Series Forecasting via Universal Correlation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30376)
*Haochen Yuan,Yichen Song,Yunbo Wang,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unicorn is a new framework for time series forecasting that balances scalability with the modeling of inter-channel dependencies through a latent prototype codebook.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the limitations in current time series models that either scale well but fail to capture inter-channel dependencies or effectively model those dependencies but struggle with generalization across varied datasets.

Method: Unicorn employs a latent prototype codebook to decouple correlation modeling from channel identities, projecting diverse channels into a shared latent space for learning reusable interaction patterns.

Result: Unicorn surpasses existing forecasting architectures, especially in few-shot transfer situations, demonstrating improved scalability and versatility across different time series datasets.

Conclusion: The framework offers a promising approach for developing multivariate time series foundation models that can generalize across heterogeneous datasets.

Abstract: Modern time series architectures face a fundamental trade-off: channel-independent models scale well with increasing data volume but ignore critical inter-channel dependencies, while channel-dependent models are expressive but remain ``dimension-bounded'', struggling to generalize across heterogeneous datasets.To bridge this gap, we introduce Unicorn (Universal Correlation Network), a framework for scalable, multi-dataset pretraining on high-dimensional time series. At the core of Unicorn is a latent prototype codebook that decouples correlation modeling from specific channel identities. By projecting heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, UniCorN learns identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns that transfer across domains with diverse dimensionalities and semantics. Extensive experiments show that Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, particularly in few-shot transfer scenarios, offering a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models.

</details>


### [292] [When LLMs Learn to Be Consistently Wrong: A Multi-Model Study of Linear Representations of Synthetic Deception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30381)
*Vahideh Zolfaghari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates deceptive alignment in AI models through synthetic dishonesty by fine-tuning transformer models. It demonstrates that early layers can effectively detect dishonesty, with notable insights into representation stability and the effects of fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the challenge of deceptive alignment in AI safety, focusing on understanding the representational basis of learned deception through synthetic dishonesty.

Method: Five transformer models are fine-tuned using LoRA on the same question distribution to create honest and deceptive variants, enabling controlled testing of dishonesty detection via linear probes and logistic regression.

Result: Linear probes detected synthetic dishonesty with high accuracy (AUC ≥ 0.99) in early layers for four models, except Pythia-1.4B, which peaked at 0.705. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalized to new subjects with negligible loss, revealing representational differences among models.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that deceptive representations can be solidified through moderate fine-tuning, with significant implications for monitoring mechanisms based on model activations.

Abstract: Deceptive alignment, in which models maintain accurate internal representations while deliberately producing false outputs, remains a central challenge in AI safety. While strategic deception is the primary long-term concern, synthetic dishonesty - induced via direct optimization on incorrect answers - provides a controlled testbed for studying the representational basis of learned deception. We introduce a multi-model paradigm in which honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) are fine-tuned using LoRA on the same question distribution. Linear probes trained on mean-pooled hidden states detect synthetic dishonesty with near-perfect AUC (greater than or equal to 0.99) as early as layers 1-3 in four architectures, while Pythia-1.4B reaches a peak of 0.705. Logistic regression probes consistently match or outperform MLP probes, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalize with near-zero loss (Delta AUC approx. 0) to held-out MMLU subjects. Late-layer representations show strong robustness to Gaussian noise, with Gemma-2 models exhibiting exceptional stability. Mechanistic analysis of Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, and calibration (ECE) reveals two regimes: representational collapse in Pythia/Llama/Qwen versus high-dimensional preservation in Gemma-2. Across all models, the dishonesty direction consolidates progressively in deeper layers, with optimal calibration (ECE less than 0.01 except Pythia) achievable in layers 1-4. These results demonstrate that robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with implications for activation-based monitoring.

</details>


### [293] [LLMs Without Deep Neural Networks: New Architecture, Benefits and Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30385)
*Vincent Granville*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper validates a newly proposed deep neural network alternative that finds optimal solutions without traditional training processes, comparing it to existing models like RBF networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To present a novel deep learning model that enhances explainability and accuracy without the extensive training typical of DNNs.

Method: The model utilizes a closed-form solution for the loss function, achieving global optimization in a single iteration.

Result: The proposed model demonstrates comparability or improvement in accuracy and explainability against traditional DNNs and RBF networks.

Conclusion: The elimination of the training phase represents a significant advancement in neural network methodology.

Abstract: The purpose of this article is to provide validation to my deep neural network alternative in the context of LLMs. Very recently, there has been a significant interest by Chinese researchers in a model called RBF network, as a substitute to standard DNNs, with increased explainability and higher accuracy. It turns out that my new model, discovered independently, is based on the exact same machinery. But with a major twist: it does not need DNN as it finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form, in one iteration, thus eliminating the tedious training step. Here I provide a high-level overview of my technology, with case study and comparison to similar methods.

</details>


### [294] [Reducing the GPU Memory Bottleneck with Lossless Compression for ML -- Extended](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30728)
*Aditya K Kamath,Arvind Krishnamurthy,Marco Canini,Simon Peter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Invariant Bit Packing (IBP), a lossless compression algorithm aimed at enhancing data transfer speeds for machine learning tasks while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to process large datasets for ML that exceed GPU memory capacity leads to reliance on PCIe for transfers, which causes bottlenecks; existing lossy compression methods complicate deployments.

Method: The study proposes lossless compression, specifically the IBP algorithm, which removes invariant bits from tensor groups to optimize data transfer and utilizes GPU-optimized decompression techniques.

Result: IBP provides significant performance improvements, achieving 74% faster GNN training, 180% faster DLRM embedding lookup, and 24% faster LLM inference compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: The proposed IBP algorithm effectively accelerates ML processes without sacrificing accuracy, thus simplifying deployment compared to lossy compression methods.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) training and inference often process data sets far exceeding GPU memory capacity, forcing them to rely on PCIe for on-demand tensor transfers, causing critical transfer bottlenecks. Lossy compression has been proposed to relieve bottlenecks but introduces workload-dependent accuracy loss, making it complex or even prohibitive to use in existing ML deployments. We explore lossless compression as an alternative that avoids this deployment complexity. We identify where lossless compression can be integrated into ML pipelines while minimizing interference with GPU execution. Based on our findings, we introduce Invariant Bit Packing (IBP), a novel lossless compression algorithm designed to minimize data transfer time for ML. IBP identifies and eliminates invariant bits across groups of tensors, improving throughput through GPU-optimized decompression that leverages warp parallelism, low-overhead bit operations, and asynchronous PCIe transfers. We provide easy-to-use APIs, showcasing them by adding IBP support to GNN training, as well as DLRM and LLM inference frameworks. IBP achieves, on average, 74% faster GNN training, 180% faster DLRM embedding lookup, and 24% faster LLM inference.

</details>


### [295] [Functional MRI Time Series Generation via Wavelet-Based Image Transform and Spectral Flow Matching for Brain Disorder Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30387)
*Hwa Hui Tew,Junn Yong Loo,Fang Yu Leong,Julia K. Lau,Ding Fan,Hernando Ombao,Raphaël C. -W. Phan,Chee Pin Tan,Chee-Ming Ting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Dual-Spectral Flow Matching (DSFM), a generative framework for high-fidelity fMRI data synthesis that overcomes challenges related to non-stationarity and physiological variations in BOLD signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance fMRI samples for data-driven analysis despite the limitations of resource-intensive data acquisition and the challenges associated with synthesizing BOLD signals.

Method: The DSFM framework utilizes a dual-transform approach, employing a discrete wavelet transform for wavelet decomposition and a discrete cosine transform for energy compaction, followed by spectral flow matching to generate conditioned representations before reconstructing time-domain signals.

Result: The proposed method successfully generates physiologically plausible BOLD signals and improves fMRI-based brain network classification performance.

Conclusion: The dual-transform strategy effectively addresses the variability in BOLD signals, preserving physiological dynamics and enhancing model performance.

Abstract: Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) provides non-invasive access to dynamic brain activity by measuring blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signals over time. However, the resource-intensive nature of fMRI acquisition limits the availability of high-fidelity samples required for data-driven brain analysis models. While modern generative models can synthesize fMRI data, they often remain challenging in replicating their inherent non-stationarity, intricate spatiotemporal dynamics, and physiological variations of raw BOLD signals. To address these challenges, we propose Dual-Spectral Flow Matching (DSFM), a novel fMRI generative framework that cascades dual frequency representation of BOLD signals with spectral flow matching. Specifically, our framework first converts BOLD signals into a wavelet decomposition map via a discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to capture globalized transient and multi-scale variations, and projects into the discrete cosine transform (DCT) space across brain regions and time to exploit localized energy compaction of low-frequency dominant BOLD coefficients. Subsequently, a spectral flow matching model is trained to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representation. The generated samples are reconstructed through inverse DCT and inverse DWT operations to recover physiologically plausible time-domain BOLD signals. This dual-transform approach imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics. Ultimately, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through improved downstream fMRI-based brain network classification. The code is available at https://github.com/htew0001/DSFM.git .

</details>


### [296] [Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior for Personalized User Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30873)
*Jabin Koo,Hoyoung Kim,Minwoo Jang,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents FedVPA-GP, a federated learning framework that personalizes Large Language Models by addressing user preference conflicts through a novel mixture prior and orthogonal loss, outperforming traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of conflicting user preferences in federated learning for aligning LLMs while maintaining privacy.

Method: The proposed FedVPA-GP employs a Federated Mixture Prior and an Orthogonal Loss to stabilize variational inference and disentangle user preferences.

Result: Experiments on the HH-RLHF dataset show that FedVPA-GP significantly outperforms traditional monolithic frameworks in managing diverse user preferences and enables dynamic adjustments.

Conclusion: Federated Variational Preference Alignment successfully enhances federated learning systems by accommodating conflicting user intents without sacrificing privacy.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving pathway for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs); however, existing frameworks typically enforce a monolithic reward model, inevitably averaging out inherently conflicting user preferences (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness). While Variational Preference Learning (VPL) offers a pathway to personalization, adapting it to decentralized settings presents a fundamental challenge: posterior collapse driven by severe local data scarcity and heterogeneity. In this paper, we propose Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior (FedVPA-GP), a framework designed to disentangle diverse preferences without compromising privacy. To stabilize variational inference, we introduce a Federated Mixture Prior that enables clients to leverage the aggregate population distribution as a dynamic prior. Furthermore, we incorporate an Orthogonal Loss that explicitly enforces the separation of preference prototypes in the latent space. Experiments on the HH-RLHF dataset demonstrate that FedVPA-GP significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, successfully disentangling conflicting user intents and enabling dynamic preference switching.

</details>


### [297] [A Novel Evaluation Metric for Unsupervised Learning in AIS-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection: MADQI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30388)
*Ismet Gocer,Zakirul Bhuiyan,Raza Hasan,Shakeel Ahmad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a framework for detecting anomalies in maritime AIS data, introducing the Maritime Anomaly Detection Quality Index (MADQI) to evaluate machine learning models without labelled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for a systematic evaluation of unsupervised anomaly detection methods in maritime datasets, as existing algorithms lack meaningful evaluation metrics.

Method: The framework utilizes Haversine distance calculations and introduces the MADQI, which comprises four metrics (ARC, PPS, SDS, and ECE) for a comprehensive evaluation of anomaly detection methods.

Result: The proposed framework achieved a MADQI score of 80.37%, with strong performance in detecting abnormal vessel behaviour; ECE and ARC showed exceptional detection capabilities with scores of 0.907 and 1.000.

Conclusion: The framework effectively evaluates unsupervised anomaly detection in AIS datasets and provides a reliable approach for identifying vessel behaviour anomalies.

Abstract: This paper introduces a new systematic framework for detecting anomalies in maritime Automatic Identification System (AIS) datasets. These anomalies include abnormal vessel behaviours related to speed, position jumps, time gaps, and turn angles. Although unsupervised learning algorithms such as Isolation Forest are widely used for detecting anomalous vessel movements, they often lack systematic and meaningful evaluation measures. To address this limitation, we propose a novel quality metric called Maritime Anomaly Detection Quality Index (MADQI). The prosed MADQI is a composite index designed to evaluate the anomaly detection performance of machine learning models without requiring labelled data. The proposed framework uses Haversine distance calculations to analyse AIS datasets and identify anomalies based on their spatial and behavioural characteristics. The proposed MADQI evaluation framework integrates four interconnected metrics: Anomaly Rate Consistency (ARC), Physical Plausibility Score (PPS), Score Distribution Separation (SDS), and Extreme Case Evidence (ECE). These metrics are combined through automatic normalisation using multi-chunk evaluation and adaptive scaling techniques. Experimental results on the AIS dataset show that the proposed framework achieved a MADQI score of 80.37%, demonstrating its effectiveness for unsupervised anomaly detection. In particular, the algorithm performed strongly in identifying abnormal vessel behaviour. Among the individual MADQI components, ECE and ARC achieved scores of 0.907 and 1.000, respectively, indicating excellent capability in detecting extreme anomalies and maintaining anomaly rate consistency. Overall, these results are encouraging and demonstrate that the proposed framework provides a reliable and meaningful approach for evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data.

</details>


### [298] [LongDS-Bench: On the Failure of Long-Horizon Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30434)
*Kewei Xu,Xiaoben Lu,Shuofei Qiao,Zihan Ding,Haoming Xu,Lei Liang,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongDS is a benchmark designed for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis, challenging agents to manage evolving analytical states across 68 tasks derived from real-world Kaggle notebooks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate agents' ability to maintain and update analytical contexts over extended interactions, which is currently untested in existing benchmarks.

Method: LongDS consists of 68 tasks across six domains, focusing on state-evolution patterns and spanning 2,225 turns, with an average dependency of 11.3 turns per task.

Result: Evaluation of five models showed the best achieving only 48.45% accuracy, indicating significant performance drops over time, particularly in long-horizon errors contributing to 52%-69% of failures.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that the primary challenge lies in maintaining correct analytical states rather than simply increasing interaction steps, and LongDS is released to facilitate further research.

Abstract: Real-world data analysis is inherently iterative, yet existing benchmarks mostly evaluate isolated or short interactive tasks, leaving agents' ability to track evolving analytical context over long horizons untested. We introduce LongDS, a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis where agents must maintain, update, restore, and compose evolving analytical states. LongDS comprises 68 tasks constructed from real-world Kaggle notebooks, spanning 2,225 turns across six domains including Geoscience, Business, and Education. Tasks are designed around state-evolution patterns (e.g., counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition), with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models, we find that the best model reaches only 48.45% average accuracy, performance drops nearly 47 points from early to late turns, and long-horizon errors account for 52%--69% of failures. Further analysis shows that additional agent steps do not necessarily improve performance, suggesting that the key bottleneck is maintaining a correct analytical state rather than increasing interaction budget. We release LongDS to support research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/DataMind.

</details>


### [299] [DG-CoLearn: An Efficient Collaborative Learning Framework for Dynamic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31427)
*Ashley Hoi-Ting Au,Zikun Zhang,Ligang He,Qiang Ni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DG-CoLearn is a framework for dynamic graph learning that improves efficiency and privacy in collaborative settings by focusing on incremental updates and protecting client data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational limitations and privacy concerns in existing dynamic graph learning methods, particularly in collaborative environments with partitioned data.

Method: DG-CoLearn employs incremental graph snapshot processing, emphasizing affected graph regions by temporal updates, and includes a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism to facilitate multi-hop message passing without revealing sensitive cross-client data.

Result: Achieved up to 33.8× speedup in training time, 27.4× reduction in communication overhead, and significant improvements in predictive performance for node classification and link prediction tasks.

Conclusion: DG-CoLearn effectively balances efficiency, scalability, and privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning environments.

Abstract: Dynamic graph learning (DGL) is essential for modelling evolving graph data, but existing methods suffer from significant computational overhead due to repeated full-snapshot retraining and are not well-suited for collaborative settings with partitioned data. In realistic graph systems, cross-partition edges are unavoidable, but direct sharing of graph structure between clients may violate privacy constraints. We propose DG-CoLearn, a client-oblivious collaborative dynamic graph learning framework built on incremental graph snapshot processing, which focuses computation on graph regions affected by temporal updates while preserving historical information through temporal modelling. This incremental design is consistently applied across the entire graph processing pipeline, including a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism to enable accurate multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DG-CoLearn achieves up to 33.8$\times$ speedup in training time and 27.4$\times$ reduction in communication overhead, while consistently improving predictive performance on both node classification (up to 13.36% F1 improvement) and link prediction (up to 8.27% MAP improvement) tasks. These results highlight the effectiveness of DG-CoLearn in bridging efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

</details>


### [300] [NumLeak: Public Numeric Benchmarks as Latent Labels in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30393)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study introduces NumLeak, a framework to assess LLM memorization vs. genuine prediction skills. Evidence shows top LLMs excel at recalling financial and economic data, but struggle with novel queries, revealing significant memorization biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether current language models possess true predictive abilities or merely recall memorized data when tested against numeric benchmarks.

Method: Introduces the NumLeak framework that combines real-world API evaluations and controlled white-box testing on a causal language model, assessing various economic indicators.

Result: Top LLMs demonstrate high recall rates for financial metrics but show a collapse in performance on novel queries, indicating heavy reliance on memorization.

Conclusion: A simple defensive prompting mechanism substantially reduces vulnerability to memorization exploits, highlighting the model's over-reliance on learned data.

Abstract: Public numeric benchmarks appear in pretraining, so an evaluation that conditions on a date may be measuring memorized recall rather than out-of-sample skill. We introduce NumLeak, a measurement framework that combines API-boundary probes on production models with a white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. Top-tier frontier LLMs recall the Fama-French market excess return at 3-seed pooled Pearson r=0.97-0.99 while staying within 0.15 within-25bps on the five sibling factors; comparable fidelity appears on U.S. unemployment, CPI inflation, and NOAA temperature. On a recent-release holdout, parse rate collapses to 21-57% but r stays at approximately 0.99 on months answered, the refuse-or-recall asymmetry a memorized channel predicts. The white-box experiment reproduces the dose-response, and logprob ranking detects memorization that open-ended generation misses, implying closed-API black-box probes understate the channel. A Sonnet "date to market-sentiment" regression that correlates with true Mkt-RF at r=0.74 collapses to r=0.02 once the model's own recall is residualized out. A one-line system-prompt defense blocks 99.8% of a non-adaptive single-turn suffix attack set at near-zero utility cost on conceptual and historical-narrative queries

</details>


### [301] [PithTrain: A Compact and Agent-Native MoE Training System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31463)
*Ruihang Lai,Hao Kang,Haozhan Tang,Akaash R. Parthasarathy,Zichun Yu,Junru Shao,Todd C. Mowry,Chenyan Xiong,Tianqi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PithTrain is an agent-native MoE training framework that enhances agent-task efficiency (ATE) while matching throughput of existing production frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high costs associated with evolving training frameworks for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures.

Method: Develop a compact, agent-native training framework (PithTrain) and introduce ATE-Bench to evaluate real-world tasks.

Result: PithTrain achieves comparable throughput to production frameworks and improves agent-task efficiency with significantly reduced Agent Turns and Active GPU Time.

Conclusion: PithTrain provides a more efficient approach to training MoE models by leveraging AI coding agents, lowering operational costs.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the dominant architecture for frontier language models. To meet this demand, production frameworks have built optimized MoE training stacks over years of engineering effort. Yet evolving these stacks for new architectures and system optimizations remains expensive. With the rise of AI coding agents, they could automate parts of training-framework development and accelerate this evolution. But applying them to these existing frameworks carries hidden costs, invisible to today's throughput-only evaluations. We name this missing dimension agent-task efficiency (ATE): the cost of using coding agents to understand, operate, and extend a framework. Grounded in four agent-native design principles, we build PithTrain, a compact, agent-native MoE training framework. We further introduce ATE-Bench, covering real-world training-framework tasks. Our evaluation shows PithTrain matches the throughput of production frameworks, and on ATE-Bench, PithTrain enables higher agent-task efficiency, with up to 62% fewer Agent Turns and 64% less Active GPU Time.

</details>


### [302] [Generalized Intention Modeling in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31318)
*Mateusz Odrowaz-Sypniewski,Jasmine Bayrooti,Ajay Shankar,Amanda Prorok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a task-adaptive opponent modeling framework that improves intent representation in multi-agent reinforcement learning by learning from multiple representations based on performance-driven criteria rather than fixed information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the inadequacy of existing opponent modeling methods, which rely on a priori chosen information that does not universally represent an opponent's intent, leading to suboptimal decision-making.

Method: The proposed approach learns a performance-driven mixture of intent representations and introduces a new intention representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego-agent's expected returns.

Result: The method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in various tasks, demonstrating improved performance in opponent modeling and providing insights into effective strategies.
